clauses.text: A person is not eligible for election or appointment to a State office unless the person is a citizen of Kenya.
A State officer or a member of the defence forces shall not hold dual citizenship.
Clauses (1) and (2) shall not apply to—

sub_clauses.text: judges and members of commissions; or
any person who has been granted citizenship of another country as a result of being a refugee.

articles.simple_explanation: Only Kenyan citizens can hold State office. State officers and defence forces members cannot hold dual citizenship (except judges and commission members).

articles.title: Legislation to establish the ethics and anti-corruption commission

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation to establish an independent ethics and anti-corruption commission, which shall be and have the status and powers of a commission under Chapter Fifteen, for purposes of ensuring compliance with, and enforcement of, the provisions of this Chapter.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament must create an independent Ethics and Anti-Corruption Commission to enforce the leadership and integrity rules in this chapter.

articles.title: Legislation on leadership

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation—

sub_clauses.text: establishing procedures and mechanisms for the effective administration of this Chapter;
prescribing the penalties, in addition to the penalties referred to in this Chapter, that may be imposed for a contravention of this Chapter;
providing for the enforcement of the penalties imposed under this Chapter and the legislation; and
providing for any other matters that are necessary for ensuring the promotion of the principles of leadership and integrity under this Chapter.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament must pass laws providing the procedures, penalties and enforcement mechanisms for leadership and integrity standards.

parts.title: Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission and Delimitation of electoral units

articles.title: General principles for the electoral system

clauses.text: The electoral system shall comply with the following principles—

sub_clauses.text: freedom of citizens to exercise their political rights under Article 38;
not more than two-thirds of the members of elective public bodies shall be of the same gender;
fair representation of persons with disabilities;
universal suffrage based on the aspiration for fair representation and equality of vote; and
free and fair elections, which are—

sub_sub_clauses.text: by secret ballot;
free from violence, intimidation, improper influence or corruption;
conducted by an independent body;
transparent; and
administered in an impartial, neutral, efficient, accurate and accountable manner.

articles.simple_explanation: Elections must be free, fair, transparent and by secret ballot. The electoral system must ensure women are not less than one-third of elected bodies, persons with disabilities are represented, and all votes are equal.

articles.title: Legislation on elections

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation to provide for—

sub_clauses.text: the registration of citizens as voters;
the conduct of elections and referenda and the regulation and efficient supervision of elections and referenda, including the nomination of candidates for elections; and
the progressive registration of citizens residing outside Kenya, and the progressive realisation of their right to vote.

clauses.text: Legislation required by clause (1) shall ensure that—

sub_clauses.text: voting in elections is simple, transparent and takes into account the special needs of—

sub_sub_clauses.text: persons with disabilities; and
other persons or groups with special needs;

sub_clauses.text: elections to the National Assembly and the Senate are conducted in the same day;
the results of the elections are announced promptly at each polling station; and
appropriate structures and mechanisms to eliminate electoral malpractice are put in place, including the safekeeping of electoral materials.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament must pass laws for voter registration, conduct of elections and referenda, and gradually allow Kenyans living abroad to vote.

articles.title: Registration as a voter

clauses.text: A person qualifies for registration as a voter at elections or referenda if the person—

sub_clauses.text: is an adult citizen;
is not declared to be of unsound mind; and
has not been convicted of an election offence during the preceding five years.

clauses.text: A citizen who qualifies for registration as a voter shall be registered at only one place as a voter.
A person shall not vote in an election or a referendum unless the person is registered as a voter.

articles.simple_explanation: Any adult Kenyan citizen who is of sound mind and has not been convicted of an election offence in the last 5 years can register as a voter. You can only be registered in one place.

articles.title: Candidates for election and political parties to comply with code of conduct

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation providing for a code of conduct for candidates and political parties contesting elections.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament must create a code of conduct for all candidates and political parties in elections.

articles.title: Eligibility to stand as an independent candidate

clauses.text: Any person is eligible to stand as an independent candidate for election if the person—

sub_clauses.text: is not a member of a registered political party and has not been a member for at least three months before the date of the election;
satisfies any educational and moral requirements prescribed by this Constitution or by an Act of Parliament under this Constitution; and
is not a member of a registered political party and has not been a member for at least three months before the date of the election.

articles.simple_explanation: Anyone can stand as an independent candidate as long as they have not been a political party member for at least 3 months before the election and meet educational and moral requirements.

articles.title: Voting

clauses.text: At every election, the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission shall ensure that—

sub_clauses.text: whatever voting method is used, the system is simple, transparent and verifiable;
the votes cast are counted, tabulated and the results announced promptly by the presiding officer at each polling station;
the results from the polling stations are openly and accurately collated and promptly announced by the returning officer; and
appropriate structures and mechanisms to eliminate electoral malpractice are put in place, including the safekeeping of electoral materials.

articles.simple_explanation: The Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission (IEBC) must ensure all voting is simple, transparent and verifiable. Results must be announced at each polling station immediately after counting.

articles.title: Electoral disputes

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation to establish mechanisms for timely settling of electoral disputes.

Petitions concerning an election, other than a presidential election, shall be filed within twenty-eight days after the declaration of the election results by the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission.
Service of a petition may be made on a respondent outside Kenya.

articles.simple_explanation: All election disputes except presidential ones must be filed in court within 28 days of results being announced. Parliament must set up mechanisms for fast resolution of election disputes.

articles.title: Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission

clauses.text: There is established the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission.
The Commission shall consist of a chairperson and eight other members appointed in accordance with the procedure provided for in the legislation contemplated in clause (9).
A member of the Commission shall not, for a period of five years after ceasing to hold office, hold any other State office.
A member of the Commission shall be a citizen of Kenya.

The Commission is responsible for conducting or supervising referenda and elections to any elective body or office established by this Constitution, and any other elections as prescribed by an Act of Parliament and, in particular, for—

sub_clauses.text: the continuous registration of citizens as voters;
the regular revision of the voters' roll;
the delimitation of constituencies and wards;
the regulation of the process by which parties nominate candidates for elections;
the settlement of electoral disputes, other than election petitions and disputes subsequent to the declaration of election results;
the registration of candidates for election;
voter education;
the facilitation of the observation, monitoring and evaluation of elections;
the regulation of money spent by a candidate or party in respect of any election;
the development of a code of conduct for candidates and parties contesting elections; and
the monitoring of compliance with the legislation required by Article 82(1)(b) relating to nomination of candidates by parties.

clauses.text: The Commission shall exercise its powers and perform its functions in accordance with this Constitution and national legislation.
A member of the Commission shall not hold any other State office.
A member of the Commission shall not be a member of a political party.
Parliament shall enact legislation to provide for—

sub_clauses.text: the qualifications of members;
nomination and appointment of the chairperson and members;
the conduct and operations of the Commission; and
the conduct of electoral processes.

articles.simple_explanation: The IEBC is an independent body responsible for running all elections and referenda, registering voters, delimiting constituencies, educating voters, and regulating political parties and campaign spending.

articles.title: Delimitation of electoral units

clauses.text: There shall be two hundred and ninety constituencies for the purposes of elections of members to the National Assembly.

Within twelve months after every national census, the Commission shall review the names and boundaries of constituencies, and of the wards within counties, and may alter them in accordance with this Article.
The number of constituencies shall not be increased or reduced and the number of wards shall not be reduced.

In reviewing constituency and ward boundaries, the Commission shall ensure that each constituency has as nearly as possible the same number of inhabitants as the other constituencies, and each ward has as nearly as possible the same number of inhabitants as the other wards within that county.
In addition to the requirement in clause (4), the Commission shall give due regard to—

sub_clauses.text: population density and demographic trends;
physical features and natural boundaries;
the infrastructure, including the means of communication and the geographical features;
community of interest, historical, economic and cultural ties; and
the need to ensure adequate representation of marginalised groups including—

sub_sub_clauses.text: persons with disabilities; and
minorities.

clauses.text: The boundaries of a constituency or ward may differ from the boundaries of an administrative unit.
The Commission shall complete a review of constituency and ward boundaries within twelve months after receiving the results of a census.
The names and details of the constituencies are as set out in the First Schedule.
If the Commission alters the boundaries of a constituency or ward, the alteration takes effect after the dissolution of Parliament first following the alteration.
All decisions of the Commission relating to the review of constituency and ward boundaries shall be made by a majority of all the members.

articles.simple_explanation: Kenya has 290 constituencies. Their boundaries are reviewed after every census by the IEBC to ensure roughly equal populations. Physical features, community ties and infrastructure must be considered.

articles.title: Allocation of party list seats

clauses.text: Elections for the seats in the National Assembly, the Senate and the county assemblies provided for by Articles 97(1)(c), 98(1)(b), (c) and (d) and 177(1)(b) and (c) shall be on the basis of proportional representation by use of party lists.
The Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission shall be responsible for the administration of the party list system and shall ensure that—

sub_clauses.text: each party list submitted by a political party comprises the appropriate number of qualified candidates;
the parties' submitted lists reflect the principle of gender equality; and
a party list submitted by a political party reflects the same composition, as nearly as practicable, of the party's overall candidate representation.

clauses.text: The seats referred to in clause (1) shall be allocated to parties in proportion to the number of seats won by each party in the relevant county assembly, the National Assembly or the Senate, as applicable, before the allocation of those seats.

articles.simple_explanation: Party list seats (the special seats for women, youth, persons with disabilities) are allocated proportionally based on how many seats each party wins in the main election.

articles.title: Basic requirements for political parties

clauses.text: Every political party shall—

sub_clauses.text: have a national character and promote national unity;
have a democratically elected governing body;
promote and uphold national cohesion and unity;
abide by the democratic principles of good governance, promote and practise democracy through regular, fair and free elections within the party;
respect the right of all persons to participate in the political process, including minorities and marginalised groups;
respect and promote human rights and fundamental freedoms, and gender equality and equity;
promote the objects and principles of this Constitution and the rule of law; and
subscribe to and observe the code of conduct for political parties.

clauses.text: A political party shall not—

sub_clauses.text: be founded on a religious, linguistic, racial, ethnic, gender or regional basis or seek to engage in advocacy of hatred on any such basis;
engage in or encourage violence by, or intimidation of, its members, supporters, opponents or any other person;
establish or maintain a paramilitary force, militia or similar organisation;
engage in bribery or other forms of corruption; or
except as is provided under this Constitution or any Act of Parliament, hold or use public resources to promote its interests or that of its members.

articles.simple_explanation: Political parties must have a national character, be democratic, promote human rights, gender equality and unity, and must not be based on religion, ethnicity, gender or region.

articles.title: Legislation on political parties

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation to provide for—

sub_clauses.text: the registration and regulation of political parties;
the general conduct of political parties;
the disclosure of sources of funding of political parties;
the public funding of political parties;
the welfare, conduct and ethical practices of members of political parties;
the enforcement of this Chapter; and
any other matters necessary for the proper management and conduct of political parties.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament must pass laws for registering parties, regulating their conduct, controlling party funding and protecting the welfare of party members.

articles.title: Establishment of Parliament

clauses.text: There is established a Parliament of Kenya, which shall consist of the National Assembly and the Senate.
The National Assembly and the Senate shall perform their respective functions as set out in this Constitution.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament consists of two Houses: the National Assembly (lower house) and the Senate (upper house). Both perform different but complementary roles.

articles.title: Role of Parliament

clauses.text: The legislative authority of the Republic is derived from the people and, at the national level, is vested in and exercised by Parliament.
Parliament manifests the diversity of the nation, represents the will of the people, and exercises their sovereignty.
Parliament may consider and pass amendments to this Constitution, and alter county boundaries only in accordance with Articles 255 to 257.
Parliament shall not delegate its legislative functions to any other body or authority.
No person or body, other than Parliament, has the power to make provision having the force of law in Kenya except under authority conferred by this Constitution or by legislation.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament makes laws on behalf of the people. Only Parliament has law-making power — it cannot delegate this. No other body can make laws unless Parliament gives it specific authority.

articles.title: Role of the National Assembly

clauses.text: The National Assembly represents the people of the constituencies and special interests in the National Assembly.
The National Assembly deliberates on and resolves issues of concern to the people.
The National Assembly enacts legislation in accordance with Part 4 of this Chapter.
The National Assembly—

sub_clauses.text: determines the allocation of national revenue between the levels of government, as provided in Part 4 of Chapter Twelve;
appropriates funds for expenditure by the national government and other national State organs; and
exercises oversight over national revenue and its expenditure.

clauses.text: The National Assembly—

sub_clauses.text: reviews the conduct in office of the President, the Deputy President and other State officers and initiates the process of removing them from office; and
exercises oversight of State organs.

clauses.text: The National Assembly approves declarations of war and extensions of states of emergency.

articles.simple_explanation: The National Assembly represents constituencies and approves national spending, revenue allocation, and holds the President and State officers accountable.

articles.title: Role of the Senate

clauses.text: The Senate represents the counties, and serves to protect the interests of the counties and their governments.
The Senate participates in the law-making function of Parliament by considering, debating and approving Bills concerning counties, as provided in Articles 109 to 113.
The Senate determines the allocation of national revenue among counties, as provided in Article 217, and exercises oversight over national revenue allocated to the county governments.

The Senate participates in the oversight of State officers by considering and determining any resolution to remove the President or Deputy President from office in the circumstances contemplated in Articles 144 and 145.

articles.simple_explanation: The Senate represents the 47 counties and protects county interests. It participates in laws affecting counties, decides revenue sharing among counties, and can remove the President.

articles.title: Membership of the National Assembly

clauses.text: The National Assembly consists of—

sub_clauses.text: two hundred and ninety members, each elected by the registered voters of single member constituencies;
forty-seven women, each elected by the registered voters of the counties, each county constituting a single member constituency;

twelve members nominated by parliamentary political parties according to their proportion of members of the National Assembly in accordance with Article 90, to represent special interests including the youth, persons with disabilities and workers; and
the Speaker, who is an ex officio member.

articles.simple_explanation: The National Assembly has 290 elected constituency members, 47 county women representatives, 12 nominated members (youth, disabled, workers), and the Speaker (ex officio).

articles.title: Membership of the Senate

clauses.text: The Senate consists of—

sub_clauses.text: forty-seven members each elected by the registered voters of the counties, each county constituting a single member constituency;
sixteen women members who shall be nominated by political parties according to their proportion of members of the Senate elected under paragraph (a) in accordance with Article 90;
two members, being one man and one woman, representing youth;
two members, being one man and one woman, representing persons with disabilities; and
the Speaker, who shall be an ex officio member.

articles.simple_explanation: The Senate has 47 elected county senators, 16 nominated women, 2 youth representatives (1 man, 1 woman), 2 disability representatives (1 man, 1 woman), and the Speaker (ex officio).

articles.title: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as member of Parliament

clauses.text: Unless disqualified under clause (2), a person is eligible for election as a member of Parliament if the person—

sub_clauses.text: is a citizen of Kenya;
satisfies any educational and moral requirements prescribed by this Constitution or by an Act of Parliament; and
is registered as a voter.

clauses.text: A person is disqualified from being elected as a member of Parliament if the person—

sub_clauses.text: is a State officer or other public officer, other than a member of Parliament;
has, at any time within the five years immediately before the date of election, held office as a member of the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission;
has not been a citizen of Kenya for at least ten years immediately preceding the date of election;
is of unsound mind;
is an undischarged bankrupt;
is subject to a sentence of imprisonment of at least six months, as of the date of registration as a candidate, or at the time of election; or
has been found, in accordance with any law, to have misused or abused a State office or public office.

articles.simple_explanation: To stand for Parliament you must be a Kenyan citizen, registered voter, meet educational and moral standards, and not be a public officer, bankrupt, of unsound mind, or serving a sentence of 6+ months.

articles.title: Promotion of representation of marginalised groups

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation to promote the representation in Parliament of—

sub_clauses.text: women;
persons with disabilities;
youth;
ethnic and other minorities; and
marginalised communities.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament must pass laws to promote the representation of women, people with disabilities, youth, ethnic minorities and marginalised groups.

articles.title: Election of members of Parliament

clauses.text: A general election of members of Parliament shall be held on the second Tuesday in August, in every fifth year.

Whenever a vacancy occurs in a seat in Parliament, the Speaker of the relevant House shall, within twenty-one days of the occurrence of the vacancy, give notice in writing to the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission of the vacancy.
A by-election to fill that vacancy shall be held within ninety days of the occurrence of the vacancy.

articles.simple_explanation: General elections for Parliament are held every 5 years on the second Tuesday of August. If a seat becomes vacant, a by-election must be held within 90 days.

articles.title: Term of Parliament

clauses.text: The term of each House of Parliament expires on the date of the next general election.
When Kenya is at war, Parliament may, by resolution supported by at least two-thirds of members of each House, from time to time extend the term of Parliament by not more than six months at a time.
The term of Parliament shall not be extended under clause (2) for a total of more than twelve months.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament's term ends on the date of the next general election. In times of war, Parliament can extend its term by up to 6 months at a time, but never more than 12 months total.

articles.title: Vacation of office of member of Parliament

clauses.text: The office of a member of Parliament becomes vacant—

sub_clauses.text: if the member dies;

if, during any session of Parliament, the member is absent from eight sittings of the relevant House without permission, in writing, of the Speaker, and is unable to offer a satisfactory explanation for the absence;
if the member is found, in accordance with any law, to have misused or abused a State office or public office;
if the member is convicted of an offence punishable by imprisonment for at least six months;
if the member is unable to perform the functions of office as a result of physical or mental illness or infirmity;
on resignation, in writing, addressed to the Speaker;
if the member ceases to be a citizen of Kenya; or
if the member is disqualified from election as a member of Parliament under this Constitution.

articles.simple_explanation: An MP loses their seat if they die, are absent for 8 sittings without permission, misuse public office, are convicted of a crime carrying 6+ months, resign, or lose citizenship.

articles.title: Right of recall

clauses.text: The electorate under Articles 97(1)(a) and (b) and 98(1)(a) have the right to recall the member of Parliament representing their constituency, ward or county before the end of the term of the relevant House of Parliament.
Parliament shall enact legislation to provide for the grounds on which a member of Parliament may be recalled and the procedure to be followed.

articles.simple_explanation: Voters have the right to recall (remove) their elected MP before the end of the term. Parliament must set out the grounds and process for recall.

articles.title: Determination of questions of membership

clauses.text: The High Court shall hear and determine any question whether—

sub_clauses.text: a person has been validly elected as a member of Parliament; or
the seat of a member has become vacant.

clauses.text: A question under clause (1) shall be heard and determined within six months of the date of lodging the petition.

articles.simple_explanation: The High Court hears disputes about whether someone was validly elected to Parliament or whether a seat has become vacant. It must decide within 6 months.

articles.title: Speakers and Deputy Speakers of Parliament

clauses.text: There shall be a Speaker and a Deputy Speaker for each House of Parliament.
Each Speaker and Deputy Speaker shall be elected by the relevant House, in accordance with the Standing Orders of that House.
A person is not qualified to be elected as Speaker of a House of Parliament unless the person qualifies to be elected as a member of that House.
A Speaker shall, before assuming the duties of office—

sub_clauses.text: take and subscribe to the oath or affirmation of office prescribed by the Third Schedule; and
if not already a member of the relevant House, take the oath or affirmation required of members.

clauses.text: The Deputy Speaker of each House of Parliament shall be a member of the relevant House.

articles.simple_explanation: Each House has a Speaker and Deputy Speaker elected by the House. The Speaker is an ex officio member (member by position, not election). Speakers must take an oath before starting their duties.

articles.title: Presiding in Parliament

clauses.text: At any sitting of a House of Parliament—

sub_clauses.text: the Speaker of that House presides;
in the absence of the Speaker, the Deputy Speaker presides; and
in the absence of both the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker, another member of the House elected by the House presides.

clauses.text: At a joint sitting of the Houses of Parliament, the Speaker of the National Assembly shall preside, and in the absence of that Speaker, the Speaker of the Senate shall preside.

articles.simple_explanation: In any sitting, the Speaker presides. If the Speaker is absent, the Deputy Speaker takes over. At joint sittings, the Speaker of the National Assembly presides.

articles.title: Party leaders

clauses.text: There shall be a leader of the majority party and a leader of the minority party in each House of Parliament.
The leader of the majority party in the National Assembly is the person who is the leader in the National Assembly of the largest party or coalition of parties.
The leader of the minority party in the National Assembly is the person who is the leader in the National Assembly of the second largest party or coalition of parties.
The leader of the majority party and the leader of the minority party in the Senate are the equivalent leaders in the Senate.

articles.simple_explanation: The leader of the biggest party or coalition in each House is the leader of the majority party. The leader of the second biggest is the leader of the minority party.

articles.title: Exercise of legislative powers

clauses.text: Parliament shall exercise its legislative power through Bills passed by Parliament and assented to by the President.
Any Bill may originate in the National Assembly.
A Bill not concerning county government is considered only in the National Assembly, and passed in accordance with this Part.
A Bill concerning county government may originate in the National Assembly or the Senate, and is passed in accordance with this Part.

A Bill may be introduced by any member or committee of the relevant House of Parliament, and, in the case of a Bill involving expenditure, may only be introduced following the recommendation of the relevant Cabinet Secretary.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament makes laws through Bills that are passed and then signed by the President. Any MP or committee can introduce a Bill, but spending Bills need a Cabinet Secretary's recommendation first.

articles.title: Bills concerning county government

clauses.text: In this Constitution, "a Bill concerning county government" means—

sub_clauses.text: a Bill containing provisions affecting the functions and powers of the county governments set out in the Fourth Schedule;
a Bill relating to the election of members of a county assembly or a county executive; or
a Bill referred to in Chapter Twelve affecting the finances of county governments.

clauses.text: A Bill concerning county governments is—

sub_clauses.text: a special Bill, which is a Bill that affects the boundaries of counties, the powers, functions or status of county governments; or
an ordinary Bill, which is any other Bill concerning county government.

clauses.text: Before either House of Parliament considers a Bill, the Speaker of the relevant House shall determine whether it is a Bill concerning county governments and, if it is, whether it is a special or an ordinary Bill.

The Speakers of both Houses of Parliament, sitting jointly, shall consider and determine any question concerning the correct categorisation of a Bill if the Speakers disagree on the categorisation of that Bill.

articles.simple_explanation: A Bill 'concerning county government' is one that affects county functions, county elections, or county finances. Such Bills can be either 'special' (affecting county boundaries/powers) or 'ordinary'.

articles.title: Special Bills concerning county governments

clauses.text: A special Bill concerning county governments may be introduced in the Senate.
A special Bill concerning county governments shall be passed by the Senate, and the National Assembly, by a majority of all their respective members.

If a special Bill is passed by the Senate but rejected or not passed, or passed with amendments, by the National Assembly within ninety days after being passed by the Senate, the Bill shall be submitted to a joint committee of the Houses.
A mediation committee reconstituted for the purposes of this clause shall be subject to the requirements of Article 113.

articles.simple_explanation: Special Bills about counties can start in the Senate and must be passed by a majority of all members of both Houses. If there's disagreement, a joint mediation committee is formed.

articles.title: Ordinary Bills concerning county governments

clauses.text: An ordinary Bill concerning county governments may be introduced in either House of Parliament.
If both Houses pass the Bill in the same form, the Bill shall be presented to the President for assent.

If the two Houses disagree on the Bill or any of its provisions, the Bill shall be referred to a mediation committee constituted as provided by Article 113 to attempt to develop a version of the Bill that both Houses will pass.

articles.simple_explanation: Ordinary Bills about counties can start in either House. If both pass it the same way, it goes to the President. If they disagree, a mediation committee is formed.

articles.title: Mediation committees

clauses.text: If a Bill is referred to a mediation committee, the committee shall—

sub_clauses.text: consist of equal numbers of members of each House of Parliament nominated by the respective parties in each House in proportion to their membership in the House; and
attempt to develop a version of the Bill that both Houses will pass.

clauses.text: If a mediation committee does not agree on a mediated version of the Bill within thirty days after the Bill is referred to it—

sub_clauses.text: the Bill is defeated if it was originally introduced in the National Assembly; or
the Bill is referred back to the Senate, if it was originally introduced in the Senate, and shall not be reintroduced in Parliament within six months after the referral back.

clauses.text: If a mediation committee agrees on a mediated version of the Bill, each House of Parliament shall vote on the mediated version within seven days after the committee has agreed on it.
If both Houses pass the mediated version of the Bill, the Bill shall be presented to the President for assent.
If either House fails to pass the mediated version—

sub_clauses.text: the Bill is defeated if it was originally introduced in the National Assembly; or
the Bill is referred back to the Senate if it was originally introduced in the Senate, and shall not be reintroduced in Parliament within six months after the referral back.

articles.simple_explanation: If a mediation committee is formed, it has 30 days to agree on a version of the Bill. If they agree, both Houses vote on it within 7 days. If they can't agree, the Bill is defeated or referred back.

articles.title: Money Bills

clauses.text: A money Bill may not deal with any matter other than those listed in the definition of 'money Bill' in clause (3).

If, in the opinion of the Speaker of the National Assembly, a motion makes provision for a matter listed in the definition of 'money Bill' in clause (3), the Assembly may proceed only in accordance with the recommendation of the relevant Cabinet Secretary.
In this Constitution, 'money Bill' means a Bill, other than a Bill specified in Article 218, that contains provisions dealing with—

sub_clauses.text: taxes;
the imposition of charges on the Consolidated Fund or other public funds;
the appropriation, receipt, custody, investment or issue of public money;
the raising or guaranteeing of any loan or its repayment; or
matters incidental to any of those matters.

articles.simple_explanation: A Money Bill is one dealing with taxes, public funds, borrowing or guarantees. It can only be introduced after the Cabinet Secretary for Finance recommends it.

articles.title: Presidential assent and referral

clauses.text: Within fourteen days after receipt of a Bill, the President shall—

sub_clauses.text: assent to the Bill; or
refer the Bill back to Parliament for reconsideration by Parliament, noting any reservations that the President has concerning the Bill.

clauses.text: If the President refers a Bill back for reconsideration, Parliament may, following the appropriate procedures under this Part—

sub_clauses.text: amend the Bill in light of the President's reservations; or
pass the Bill a second time without amendment.

clauses.text: If Parliament, after considering the President's reservations, passes the Bill a second time—

sub_clauses.text: with amendments that fully accommodate the President's reservations, the President shall assent to the amended Bill within seven days after the Bill is presented to the President; or

without amendments, or with amendments that do not fully accommodate the President's reservations, the President shall either assent to the Bill or refer it to the Supreme Court within seven days for an advisory opinion on the Bill's constitutionality.

clauses.text: If the Supreme Court advises that the Bill is not inconsistent with this Constitution, the President shall assent to it within seven days of that advice.
If the Supreme Court advises that the Bill is inconsistent with this Constitution, the President shall decline to assent to it.

If the President does not act as required by this Article, the Bill takes effect as if the President had assented to it on the last day by which the President was required to assent to, or refer, the Bill.

articles.simple_explanation: The President has 14 days to sign a Bill into law or refer it back to Parliament with concerns. If Parliament passes it again, the President must sign it or take it to the Supreme Court.

articles.title: Coming into force of laws

clauses.text: A Bill passed by Parliament and assented to by the President shall be published in the Gazette as an Act of Parliament within seven days after assent.

Subject to clause (3), an Act of Parliament comes into force on the fourteenth day after its publication in the Gazette, unless the Act stipulates a different date on or time at which it comes into force.
If an Act of Parliament stipulates that it shall come into force on a date to be fixed by a notice in the Gazette, the Act comes into force on that date.

articles.simple_explanation: Once signed, a law is published in the Gazette within 7 days and comes into force 14 days later, unless the law itself sets a different date.

articles.title: Powers, privileges and immunities

clauses.text: There shall be freedom of speech and debate in Parliament and before its committees, and that freedom shall not be impeached or questioned in any court or otherwise.
Parliament shall, by legislation, provide for the powers, privileges and immunities of Parliament, its committees and members.

articles.simple_explanation: MPs have freedom of speech in Parliament — nothing said during debates can be used against them in court. Parliament sets its own rules on powers, privileges and immunities.

articles.title: Public access and participation

clauses.text: Parliament shall—

sub_clauses.text: conduct its business in an open manner, and its sittings and those of its committees shall be open to the public; and
facilitate public participation and involvement in the legislative and other business of Parliament and its committees.

clauses.text: Parliament may not exclude the public from a sitting of a committee unless in exceptional circumstances the relevant Speaker considers that there are justifiable reasons for the exclusion.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament must conduct its business openly. The public can attend all sittings and committee meetings. Parliament must encourage public participation in law-making.

articles.title: Right to petition Parliament

clauses.text: Every person has a right to petition Parliament to consider any matter within its authority, including to enact, amend or repeal any legislation.
Parliament shall make provision for the procedure for the exercise of this right.

articles.simple_explanation: Every person has the right to petition Parliament asking it to make, change or repeal a law.

articles.title: Official languages of Parliament

clauses.text: The official languages of Parliament are Kiswahili and English and the business of Parliament may be conducted in English, Kiswahili or both.
The Speaker of the relevant House shall determine the language of business.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament can conduct business in either Kiswahili or English. The relevant Speaker decides which language is used.

articles.title: Quorum

clauses.text: The quorum of the National Assembly is fifty members, and the quorum of the Senate is fifteen members. The numbers do not include the Speaker.

articles.simple_explanation: At least 50 members of the National Assembly and 15 members of the Senate must be present (excluding the Speaker) for a sitting to proceed — this is called a quorum.

articles.title: Voting in Parliament

clauses.text: Except as otherwise provided in this Constitution, any question proposed for decision in either House of Parliament shall be determined by a majority of the members in the House, voting in person.
On a question proposed for decision in either House—

sub_clauses.text: the Speaker or person presiding has a casting vote; and
in the case of a tie, the question is lost.

articles.simple_explanation: Decisions in Parliament are made by a simple majority of members voting in person. If there's a tie, the matter is lost. The Speaker only votes to break a tie.

articles.title: Decisions of Senate

clauses.text: On a matter other than a matter described in clause (2), each senator has one vote.
When the Senate is to vote on—

sub_clauses.text: a Bill or amendment to a Bill that includes matters affecting county governments; or
any other matter that affects or is in the interests of county governments,

clauses.text: ...the Senate shall vote by counties, with each county having one vote, and the vote of each county being determined by majority vote of the senators from that county.

articles.simple_explanation: On most matters, each Senator has one vote. On matters affecting counties, the Senate votes by county — each county gets one vote determined by majority of its senators.

articles.title: Committees and Standing Orders

clauses.text: Each House of Parliament may establish committees, and shall make Standing Orders for the orderly conduct of its proceedings and those of its committees.
The proceedings of either House are not invalid just because of a vacancy in its membership.
Parliament shall jointly establish committees as required by the Senate and National Assembly through their Standing Orders.
For the purposes of this Article, Parliament may jointly regulate the procedure of joint committees.

articles.simple_explanation: Each House can create committees and must make Standing Orders (rules) for how it runs its business. Joint committees between the two Houses are also allowed.

articles.title: Power to call for evidence

clauses.text: Either House of Parliament, and any of its committees, has power to summon any person to appear before it for the purpose of giving evidence or providing information.
For the purposes of clause (1), either House of Parliament has the same powers as the High Court to—

sub_clauses.text: enforce the attendance of witnesses and examine them on oath, affirmation or otherwise;
compel the production of documents; and
issue a commission or request to examine witnesses abroad.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament and its committees can summon any person to give evidence or provide information, just like the High Court can.

articles.title: Location of sittings of Parliament

clauses.text: Parliament shall sit in Nairobi, but may from time to time sit elsewhere.
Parliament shall determine the time of its sittings.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament sits in Nairobi but can sit elsewhere. Parliament sets its own sitting times.

articles.title: Parliamentary Service Commission

clauses.text: There is established the Parliamentary Service Commission.
The Commission consists of—

sub_clauses.text: the Speaker of the National Assembly, who is the chairperson;
the Speaker of the Senate, who is the deputy chairperson;
seven members appointed from each House in accordance with their Standing Orders, of whom—

sub_sub_clauses.text: not fewer than two shall be women;
not fewer than two shall be men; and
not fewer than two shall be from the Senate; and

sub_clauses.text: one person nominated by the President and approved by the National Assembly.

clauses.text: The Commission is responsible for—

sub_clauses.text: providing services and facilities to ensure the efficient and effective functioning of Parliament;
constituting offices in the parliamentary service and appointing and supervising office holders;
preparing estimates of expenditure of the parliamentary service, and submitting them to the National Assembly for approval;
undertaking, singly or jointly with other relevant organisations, programmes to promote the ideals of parliamentary democracy; and
performing any other function—

sub_sub_clauses.text: necessary for the well-being of members of Parliament; or
prescribed by national legislation.

articles.simple_explanation: The Parliamentary Service Commission manages the running of Parliament — providing services, hiring staff, preparing budgets and promoting parliamentary democracy.

articles.title: Clerks and staff of Parliament

clauses.text: There shall be a Clerk for each House of Parliament, appointed by the Parliamentary Service Commission with the approval of the relevant House.
The offices of the Clerks and offices of members of the staff of Parliament are offices in the parliamentary service.

articles.simple_explanation: Each House has a Clerk appointed by the Parliamentary Service Commission. Clerks and parliamentary staff are part of the parliamentary service.

parts.title: Principles and structure of the National Executive

articles.title: Principles of executive authority

clauses.text: Executive authority derives from the people of Kenya and shall be exercised in accordance with this Constitution.
Executive authority shall be exercised in a manner compatible with the principle of service to the people of Kenya, and for their well-being and benefit.

articles.simple_explanation: Executive (government) power comes from the people and must be exercised to serve and benefit them.

articles.title: The National Executive

clauses.text: The national executive of the Republic comprises the President, the Deputy President and the rest of the Cabinet.
The composition of the national executive shall reflect the regional and ethnic diversity of the people of Kenya.

articles.simple_explanation: The national executive consists of the President, Deputy President and the Cabinet. Its composition must reflect Kenya's regional and ethnic diversity.

articles.title: Authority of the President

clauses.text: The President—

sub_clauses.text: is the Head of State and Government;
exercises the executive authority of the Republic, with the assistance of the Deputy President and Cabinet Secretaries;
is the Commander-in-Chief of the Kenya Defence Forces;
is the chairperson of the National Security Council; and
is a symbol of national unity.

clauses.text: The President shall uphold and safeguard this Constitution and the law; protect the sovereignty of the Republic; promote and enhance the unity of the nation; promote respect for the diversity of the people and communities of Kenya; and ensure the protection of human rights and fundamental freedoms and the rule of law.
The President shall not hold any other State or public office.

articles.simple_explanation: The President is the Head of State and Government, Commander-in-Chief of the Defence Forces, chairperson of the National Security Council and a symbol of national unity. The President must not hold any other office.

articles.title: Functions of the President

clauses.text: The President shall—

sub_clauses.text: address the opening of each newly elected Parliament;
address a special sitting of Parliament once every year and deliver a State of the Nation address;
submit, once every year, a report to the National Assembly on all the steps taken to ensure the progressive realisation of the rights guaranteed under Article 43; and
appoint, with the approval of the National Assembly—

sub_sub_clauses.text: the Cabinet Secretaries, in accordance with Article 152;
the Attorney-General, in accordance with Article 156;
the Secretary to the Cabinet, in accordance with Article 154;
Principal Secretaries, in accordance with Article 155;
High Commissioners, Ambassadors and diplomatic and consular representatives; and
in accordance with this Constitution, any other State or public officer whom this Constitution requires or empowers the President to appoint.

clauses.text: The President shall nominate, and with the approval of the National Assembly, appoint—

sub_clauses.text: the Director-General of the National Intelligence Service;
the Chief of the Kenya Defence Forces;
the three Service Commanders.

clauses.text: The President may—

sub_clauses.text: perform any other executive function provided for in this Constitution or in national legislation; and
exercise any other power conferred on the President by this Constitution.

clauses.text: The President shall exercise the powers conferred by this Article on the advice of the relevant body or authority, except that the President may act on personal initiative in—

sub_clauses.text: the appointment of the Deputy President;
the nomination of the Cabinet Secretaries;
the dismissal of a Cabinet Secretary;
the declaration of a state of emergency under Article 58; and
the declaration of war under clause (4)(e) of Article 132.

articles.simple_explanation: The President opens Parliament, gives annual State of the Nation addresses, reports on social rights progress, appoints Cabinet Secretaries, Ambassadors and key public officers with National Assembly approval.

articles.title: Power of mercy

clauses.text: On the petition of any person, the President may exercise a power of mercy in accordance with the advice of the Advisory Committee constituted under clause (2), and—

sub_clauses.text: grant a free or conditional pardon to a person convicted of an offence;
postpone the carrying out of a punishment, either for a specified or indefinite period;
substitute a less severe form of punishment for a punishment imposed on a person; or
remit the whole or part of a punishment imposed on a person or of a penalty or forfeiture otherwise due to the Government because of an offence.

clauses.text: There is established an Advisory Committee on the Power of Mercy, which shall consist of—

sub_clauses.text: the Attorney-General;
the Cabinet Secretary responsible for correctional services; and
at least five other members appointed by the President, none of whom shall be a State officer or in public service.

clauses.text: A member of the Committee is appointed for a term of three years and is eligible for re-appointment once.
The Committee shall consider the case of any convicted person who petitions the President for mercy, and shall advise the President on the exercise of the power of mercy.

articles.simple_explanation: The President can grant pardons, reduce sentences, or remit fines on the advice of the Advisory Committee on the Power of Mercy. Anyone can petition the President for mercy.

articles.title: Exercise of presidential powers during temporary incumbency

clauses.text: When the President is temporarily unable to perform the functions of the office of President, the Deputy President shall act as the President during that period.
A person performing the functions of the office of President under this Article shall not perform the following functions unless the incapacity of the President is likely to be prolonged—

sub_clauses.text: nominate or appoint a person to a State office; or
revoke a nomination or appointment.

articles.simple_explanation: When the President is temporarily unable to work, the Deputy President acts as President. The acting President cannot make major appointments unless the incapacity is expected to last long.

articles.title: Decisions of the President

clauses.text: A decision of the President in the exercise of any power under this Constitution shall be in writing and shall bear the seal and signature of the President.

articles.simple_explanation: Every presidential decision must be in writing and bear the President's seal and signature.

articles.title: Election of the President

clauses.text: The President shall be elected by registered voters in a national election conducted in accordance with this Constitution and any Act of Parliament regulating presidential elections.
An election of the President shall be held—

sub_clauses.text: on the same day as a general election of members of Parliament, being the second Tuesday in August, in every fifth year; or
in the circumstances described in Articles 138(5), 146(2)(b), and 150(3)(b).

articles.simple_explanation: The President is elected by registered voters in a national election held every 5 years on the same day as general elections for Parliament.

articles.title: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as President

clauses.text: A person qualifies for nomination as a presidential candidate if the person—

sub_clauses.text: is a citizen of Kenya by birth;
is qualified to stand for election as a member of the National Assembly;
is nominated by a registered political party, or is an independent candidate who satisfies the requirements of section 85; and
is nominated by not fewer than two thousand voters from each of a majority of the counties.

clauses.text: A person is not eligible for election as President if the person—

sub_clauses.text: owes allegiance to a foreign government; or
is a member of Parliament or of a county assembly.

articles.simple_explanation: To be President you must be a Kenyan citizen by birth, qualified to be an MP, nominated by a registered party or as an independent, and supported by at least 2,000 voters from a majority of counties.

articles.title: Procedure at presidential election

clauses.text: If only one candidate for President is nominated, that candidate shall be declared elected.
If two or more candidates for President are nominated, an election shall be held in each constituency.
A candidate shall be declared elected as President if the candidate receives—

sub_clauses.text: more than half of all the votes cast in the election; and
at least twenty-five per cent of the votes cast in each of more than half of the counties.

clauses.text: If no candidate is elected, a fresh election shall be held within thirty days after the previous election.
The two candidates who received the greatest number of votes in the previous election shall be the only candidates in a fresh election.
The candidate who receives the greater number of votes in a fresh election shall be declared elected.

articles.simple_explanation: A presidential candidate wins if they get more than 50% of votes cast AND at least 25% of votes in more than half of the counties. If no one wins, a run-off is held within 30 days between the top two candidates.

articles.title: Death before assuming office

clauses.text: If a presidential candidate dies before voting in a presidential election, voting shall be postponed for a period not exceeding sixty days.
If a President-elect dies after election but before assuming office, the Deputy President-elect shall assume office as President on the day on which the President-elect would have assumed office.

articles.simple_explanation: If a presidential candidate dies before voting, the election is postponed by up to 60 days. If the President-elect dies before taking office, the Deputy President-elect assumes the presidency.

articles.title: Questions as to validity of presidential election

clauses.text: A person may file a petition in the Supreme Court to question the validity of the election of the President-elect within seven days after the date of the declaration of the results of the presidential election.
Within fourteen days after the filing of a petition under clause (1), the Supreme Court shall hear and determine the petition and its decision shall be final.
If the Supreme Court determines the election of the President-elect to be invalid, a fresh election shall be held within sixty days after the determination.

articles.simple_explanation: A presidential election result can be challenged at the Supreme Court within 7 days. The Supreme Court must rule within 14 days. If the election is declared invalid, a fresh election is held within 60 days.

articles.title: Assumption of office of President

clauses.text: The swearing-in ceremony of the President-elect shall be conducted in public before the Chief Justice or, in the absence of the Chief Justice, the Deputy Chief Justice.
The President-elect shall assume office by taking and subscribing—

sub_clauses.text: the oath or affirmation of allegiance; and
the oath or affirmation for the execution of the functions of office.

articles.simple_explanation: The President-elect is sworn in publicly before the Chief Justice. They take oaths of allegiance and of office.

articles.title: Term of office of President

clauses.text: The President shall hold office for a term beginning on the date on which the President was sworn in and ending when the person next elected President in accordance with Article 136(2)(a) is sworn in.
A person shall not hold office as President for more than two terms.

articles.simple_explanation: The President serves one 5-year term. A person cannot be President for more than two terms.

articles.title: Protection from legal proceedings

clauses.text: Criminal proceedings shall not be instituted or continued in any court against a person while they are the President.
Civil proceedings shall not be instituted in any court against a person while they are the President in respect of anything done or not done in the exercise of their powers under this Constitution.

Where a provision of this Constitution requires the President to seek the approval of the National Assembly before taking any action, the period of time between the decision to take such action and the approval of the National Assembly shall not count.
The period during which a person is President shall not count in the period within which legal proceedings may be instituted against that person.

articles.simple_explanation: No criminal case can be started or continued against a sitting President. Civil cases related to their official duties also cannot be brought while they are in office. The time they are President does not count in legal time limits.

articles.title: Removal of President on grounds of incapacity

clauses.text: A member of the National Assembly, supported by at least a quarter of all the members, may move a motion for the investigation of the President's physical or mental capacity to perform the functions of office.
If a motion under clause (1) is supported by a majority of all the members of the National Assembly—

sub_clauses.text: the Speaker of the National Assembly shall inform the Speaker of the Senate and the Chief Justice; and
the Chief Justice shall appoint a tribunal of three persons who are qualified to practice medicine under any law and who have been so qualified for at least fifteen years.

clauses.text: The tribunal shall inquire into the matter and, within fourteen days after the motion is passed in the National Assembly, report to the Chief Justice and the Speakers of both Houses.

If the tribunal reports that the President is capable of performing the functions of office, the Speaker of the National Assembly shall announce that result to the Assembly and no further proceedings shall be taken.

If the tribunal reports that the President is incapable of performing the functions of office, the National Assembly shall vote on whether to pass a resolution requiring the President to vacate office.
If the National Assembly resolves by a vote of two-thirds of all its members that the President is permanently incapable of performing the functions of office, the President shall vacate office.

articles.simple_explanation: If at least a quarter of National Assembly members raise concerns about the President's mental or physical capacity, the matter is investigated by a medical tribunal. If the President is found incapable, two-thirds of the National Assembly must vote to remove them.

articles.title: Removal of President by impeachment

clauses.text: A member of the National Assembly, supported by at least a third of all the members, may move a motion for the impeachment of the President—

sub_clauses.text: on the grounds of a gross violation of a provision of this Constitution or of any other law;
where there are serious reasons for believing that the President has committed a crime under national or international law; or
for gross misconduct.

clauses.text: If a motion under clause (1) is supported by at least two-thirds of all the members of the National Assembly—

sub_clauses.text: the Speaker of the National Assembly shall inform the Speaker of the Senate of that resolution within two days; and
the Senate shall investigate the matter.

clauses.text: The President shall have the right to appear and be represented before the Senate during its investigations.

If, after the investigation, the Senate is satisfied that the particulars of any allegation against the President have been established, it shall pass a resolution supported by at least two-thirds of all members of the Senate to remove the President.
The President shall cease to hold office on the date the Senate passes the resolution.

articles.simple_explanation: A third of National Assembly members can start impeachment proceedings. If two-thirds of the Assembly agree, the Senate investigates. If two-thirds of the Senate agrees, the President is removed.

articles.title: Vacancy in the office of President

clauses.text: The office of President shall become vacant if the holder of the office—

sub_clauses.text: dies;
resigns, in writing, addressed to the Speaker of the National Assembly; or
is otherwise removed from office under this Constitution.

clauses.text: If a vacancy in the office of President occurs—

sub_clauses.text: the Deputy President shall assume the office of President for the remainder of the term; and
the person who assumes the office of President under paragraph (a) shall, as soon as reasonably practicable, nominate a person to serve as Deputy President subject to Article 150.

clauses.text: A person who assumes the office of President under this Article and who has, before that assumption of office, already served as President for a term shall not serve as President for more than a further full term.

articles.simple_explanation: The presidency becomes vacant on death, resignation or removal. The Deputy President then becomes President and must nominate a new Deputy President.

articles.title: Functions of the Deputy President

clauses.text: The Deputy President is the principal assistant of the President and shall assist the President in the exercise of executive functions.
The Deputy President shall perform the functions conferred by this Constitution and any functions of the President as directed by the President.
The Deputy President shall not hold any other State or public office.

articles.simple_explanation: The Deputy President is the President's principal assistant and performs whatever functions the President assigns. The Deputy President cannot hold any other office.

articles.title: Election and swearing-in of Deputy President

clauses.text: Each candidate in a presidential election shall nominate a person who is qualified for nomination for election as President as a candidate for Deputy President.
A candidate for Deputy President shall be—

sub_clauses.text: nominated by a presidential candidate and not independently elected; and
elected as Deputy President by election of the presidential candidate who nominated him or her.

clauses.text: The Deputy President-elect shall assume office by taking and subscribing the oath or affirmation of allegiance, and the oath or affirmation for the execution of the functions of office, before the Chief Justice or the Deputy Chief Justice.
A person is not qualified for nomination as a candidate for Deputy President if the person is disqualified from election as President.

articles.simple_explanation: The Deputy President is nominated by the presidential candidate before the election — not elected separately. They take their own oath before the Chief Justice.

articles.title: Vacancy in the office of Deputy President

clauses.text: The office of the Deputy President shall become vacant if the holder of the office—

sub_clauses.text: dies;
resigns, in writing, addressed to the President;
is removed from office under this Constitution; or
assumes the office of President.

clauses.text: If the office of the Deputy President becomes vacant, the President shall, within fourteen days, nominate a person qualified under Article 137 to the National Assembly for vetting and approval.
If the National Assembly approves the nomination, the Deputy President shall be sworn in as soon as practicable.

articles.simple_explanation: If the Deputy President's office becomes vacant, the President nominates a replacement within 14 days, subject to National Assembly approval.

articles.title: Removal of Deputy President

clauses.text: The Deputy President may be removed from office—

sub_clauses.text: on the grounds set out in Article 145(1); or
on the ground of incapacity to perform the functions of the office.

clauses.text: For removal of the Deputy President, the provisions of Article 145 or 144, as the case may be, shall apply.

articles.simple_explanation: The Deputy President can be removed through the same processes as the President — impeachment or incapacity.

articles.title: Remuneration and benefits of President and Deputy President

clauses.text: The remuneration and benefits of the President and the Deputy President shall be a charge on the Consolidated Fund.
The remuneration and benefits of the President and the Deputy President shall not be varied to their disadvantage while in office.
A former President and a former Deputy President are entitled to the benefits prescribed by an Act of Parliament.

articles.simple_explanation: The President's and Deputy President's pay comes from the Consolidated Fund and cannot be reduced while they are in office. Former holders get benefits set by Parliament.

articles.title: Cabinet

clauses.text: The Cabinet consists of—

sub_clauses.text: the President;
the Deputy President;
the Attorney-General; and
the Cabinet Secretaries.

clauses.text: The President shall nominate and, with the approval of the National Assembly, appoint Cabinet Secretaries.
A Cabinet Secretary shall not be a Member of Parliament.
The President shall appoint at least one but not more than two Cabinet Secretaries from among persons who are not citizens of Kenya.
The number of Cabinet Secretaries shall not exceed twenty-two.
A Cabinet Secretary shall—

sub_clauses.text: be a citizen of Kenya;
hold a degree from a university recognised in Kenya; and
satisfy any educational and moral requirements prescribed by this Constitution or by an Act of Parliament.

clauses.text: The President shall dismiss a Cabinet Secretary who—

sub_clauses.text: has violated the Constitution; or
the President has reasonable cause to believe has committed a criminal offence.

articles.simple_explanation: The Cabinet consists of the President, Deputy President, Attorney-General and Cabinet Secretaries (maximum 22). Cabinet Secretaries must be citizens, hold a university degree, and cannot be MPs.

articles.title: Decisions, responsibility and accountability of the Cabinet

clauses.text: Cabinet decisions shall be in writing.
Cabinet Secretaries are accountable individually, and collectively, to the President for the exercise of their powers and the performance of their functions.

A Cabinet Secretary shall attend before a committee of the National Assembly, or the Senate, when required to do so, and answer any question concerning a matter for which the Cabinet Secretary is responsible.
Cabinet Secretaries shall act in accordance with the direction of the President.

articles.simple_explanation: Cabinet decisions must be in writing. Cabinet Secretaries are accountable to the President and must appear before Parliament when called to account for their ministries.

articles.title: Secretary to the Cabinet

clauses.text: There is established the office of Secretary to the Cabinet, which shall be an office in the public service.
The Secretary to the Cabinet—

sub_clauses.text: shall be nominated by the President and, with the approval of the National Assembly, appointed by the President;
shall have the qualifications prescribed by an Act of Parliament; and
may be dismissed by the President.

clauses.text: The Secretary to the Cabinet shall—

sub_clauses.text: be the head of the public service;
supervise the administration of all Ministries and departments; and
convene and keep minutes of Cabinet meetings.

articles.simple_explanation: The Secretary to the Cabinet is appointed by the President with National Assembly approval. They are the head of the public service and run Cabinet meetings.

articles.title: Principal Secretaries

clauses.text: There shall be a Principal Secretary for each Ministry.
A Principal Secretary shall be—

sub_clauses.text: nominated by the President from among persons recommended by the Public Service Commission; and
with the approval of the National Assembly, appointed by the President.

clauses.text: A person shall not be appointed as a Principal Secretary unless the person—

sub_clauses.text: is a citizen of Kenya; and
satisfies the requirements of Chapter Six.

articles.simple_explanation: Each Ministry has a Principal Secretary nominated by the President from people recommended by the Public Service Commission, with National Assembly approval.

articles.title: Attorney-General

clauses.text: There is established the office of Attorney-General.
The Attorney-General—

sub_clauses.text: is the principal legal adviser to the Government;
shall represent the national government in court or in any other legal proceedings to which the national government is a party, other than criminal proceedings; and
shall perform any other functions conferred on the office by an Act of Parliament or by the President.

clauses.text: The Attorney-General shall have authority, with the leave of the court, to appear as a friend of the court in any civil proceedings to which the Government is not a party.
The Attorney-General shall be nominated by the President and, with the approval of the National Assembly, appointed by the President.
The Attorney-General shall be a person who has the qualifications specified in Article 166(2)(a) and (b), except for Article 166(2)(b)(i) and (ii).
The Attorney-General may resign from office by giving notice, in writing, to the President.

articles.simple_explanation: The Attorney-General is the government's chief legal adviser, represents the government in civil cases and can appear in any civil case as a friend of the court. They are appointed by the President with National Assembly approval.

articles.title: Director of Public Prosecutions

clauses.text: There is established the office of Director of Public Prosecutions.
The Director of Public Prosecutions shall be nominated and, with the approval of the National Assembly, appointed by the President.

The Director of Public Prosecutions has power to direct the Inspector-General of the National Police Service to investigate any information or allegation of criminal conduct, and the Inspector-General shall comply with any such direction.
The Director of Public Prosecutions shall have power to—

sub_clauses.text: institute and undertake criminal proceedings against any person before any court (other than a court martial) in respect of any offence alleged to have been committed;

take over and continue any criminal proceedings commenced in any court (other than a court martial) that have been instituted or undertaken by another person or authority, with the permission of the court; and

subject to clauses (5) and (6), discontinue at any stage before judgment is delivered any criminal proceedings instituted by the Director of Public Prosecutions or taken over by the Director of Public Prosecutions under paragraph (b).

clauses.text: If the discontinuation of any proceedings under clause (4)(c) takes place after the close of the prosecution's case, the defendant shall be acquitted.

The Director of Public Prosecutions shall not require the consent of any person or authority for the commencement of criminal proceedings and in the exercise of his or her powers or functions, shall not be under the direction or control of any person or authority.
The powers of the Director of Public Prosecutions may be exercised in person or by officers authorised by the Director of Public Prosecutions in accordance with general or special instructions.

When the Director of Public Prosecutions discontinues a prosecution, the Director of Public Prosecutions shall inform the victim, and where possible the complainant, of the reasons for the discontinuation.

The Director of Public Prosecutions shall, in exercising the functions of that office, have regard to the public interest, the interests of the administration of justice and the need to prevent and avoid abuse of the legal process.
Parliament shall enact legislation conferring powers of prosecution on authorities other than the Director of Public Prosecutions.
The Director of Public Prosecutions shall be a person who has the qualifications prescribed in Article 166(2)(a) and (b), except for Article 166(2)(b)(i) and (ii).
Subject to this Article, the Director of Public Prosecutions shall exercise the powers vested by Article 157(4) and (6) and in doing so, may have regard to the need for devolution.

articles.simple_explanation: The Director of Public Prosecutions (DPP) has the power to start, take over or stop criminal cases. The DPP is independent and cannot be directed by anyone. They must inform victims when a case is dropped.

articles.title: Removal and resignation of Director of Public Prosecutions

clauses.text: The Director of Public Prosecutions may resign from office by giving notice, in writing, to the President.
The Director of Public Prosecutions may be removed from office only on the grounds of—

sub_clauses.text: inability to perform the functions of office arising from mental or physical incapacity;
a breach of a provision of this Constitution; or
gross misconduct.

clauses.text: A person desiring the removal of the Director of Public Prosecutions on any ground specified in clause (2) may present a petition to the National Assembly setting out the alleged facts constituting that ground.

The National Assembly shall consider the petition and, if it is satisfied that it discloses a ground under clause (2), shall pass a resolution supported by at least two-thirds of all the members of the National Assembly directing the President to suspend the Director of Public Prosecutions and to appoint a tribunal.

articles.simple_explanation: The DPP can resign or be removed for incapacity, breach of the Constitution or gross misconduct. Removal requires two-thirds of the National Assembly to support it.

articles.title: Judicial authority

clauses.text: Judicial authority is derived from the people and vests in, and shall be exercised by, the courts and tribunals established by or under this Constitution.
In exercising judicial authority, the courts and tribunals shall be guided by the following principles—

sub_clauses.text: justice shall be done to all, irrespective of status;
justice shall not be delayed;
alternative forms of dispute resolution including reconciliation, mediation, arbitration and traditional dispute resolution mechanisms shall be promoted, subject to clause (3);
justice shall be administered without undue regard to procedural technicalities; and
the purpose and principles of this Constitution shall be protected and promoted.

clauses.text: Traditional dispute resolution mechanisms shall not be used in a way that—

sub_clauses.text: contravenes the Bill of Rights;
is repugnant to justice and morality or results in outcomes that are repugnant to justice or morality; or
is inconsistent with this Constitution or any written law.

articles.simple_explanation: Court authority comes from the people. Courts must ensure justice is done for everyone regardless of status, without delays, and without unnecessary technical hurdles. Traditional dispute resolution methods are allowed but must not violate the Constitution or the Bill of Rights.

articles.title: Independence of the Judiciary

clauses.text: In the exercise of judicial authority, the Judiciary, as constituted by Article 161, shall be subject only to this Constitution and the law and shall not be subject to the control or direction of any person or authority.
The offices of the Chief Justice, the Deputy Chief Justice, and all other judges shall not be abolished while there is a substantive holder of the office.
The remuneration and benefits payable to or in respect of a judge shall be a charge on the Consolidated Fund.

Subject to Article 168(6), the remuneration and benefits of a judge shall not be varied to the disadvantage of that judge, and the retirement benefits of a retired judge shall not be varied to the disadvantage of the retired judge during the lifetime of that retired judge.
A member of the Judiciary shall not perform any other functions or hold any position, whether public or private, to the extent that doing so compromises or prejudices the person's judicial functions.
Neither the salary and benefits of a judicial officer nor the administrative expenses of a court shall be charged to a local authority.

articles.simple_explanation: Courts and judges are independent. They only follow the Constitution and the law — no person or government body can direct or control them. Judges' salaries are protected and cannot be cut while they serve.

articles.title: Judicial offices and officers

clauses.text: The Judiciary consists of—

sub_clauses.text: the judges of the superior courts, namely the Chief Justice, the Deputy Chief Justice and the judges of the Supreme Court, the Court of Appeal, the High Court, and other courts established under clause (2)(b)(ii) and (iii); and
the magistrates, Kadhi's and other judicial officers and staff of the courts.

clauses.text: There is established the office of Chief Justice, who shall be the Head of the Judiciary.
There is established the office of Deputy Chief Justice, who shall assist the Chief Justice and deputise for the Chief Justice in the Chief Justice's absence.
The Chief Justice and the Deputy Chief Justice shall be the President and Deputy President of the Supreme Court.

articles.simple_explanation: The Judiciary consists of the Chief Justice, Deputy Chief Justice, judges of the Supreme Court, Court of Appeal, High Court and other courts, as well as magistrates, Kadhis and other judicial officers.

articles.title: System of courts

clauses.text: The superior courts are the Supreme Court, the Court of Appeal, the High Court and the courts mentioned in clause (2).
Parliament shall establish courts with the status of the High Court to hear and determine disputes relating to—

sub_clauses.text: employment and labour relations; and
the environment and the use and occupation of, and title to, land.

clauses.text: The subordinate courts are the courts established under Article 169, or by Parliament in accordance with that Article.
