articles.simple_explanation: The superior courts are the Supreme Court, Court of Appeal and High Court. Parliament must also set up specialised courts for employment disputes and land/environment disputes. Lower courts are called subordinate courts.

articles.title: Supreme Court

clauses.text: There is established the Supreme Court, which shall consist of—

sub_clauses.text: the Chief Justice, who shall be the president of the court;
the Deputy Chief Justice, who shall be the deputy president of the court; and
five other judges.

clauses.text: The Supreme Court shall have—

sub_clauses.text: exclusive original jurisdiction to hear and determine disputes relating to the elections to the office of President arising under Article 140; and
subject to clause (3) and (4), appellate jurisdiction to hear and determine appeals from—

sub_sub_clauses.text: the Court of Appeal; and
any other court or tribunal as prescribed by national legislation.

clauses.text: Appeals shall lie from the Court of Appeal to the Supreme Court—

sub_clauses.text: as of right in any case involving the interpretation or application of this Constitution; and
in any other case in which the Supreme Court, or the Court of Appeal, certifies that a matter of general public importance is involved, subject to such conditions as may be specified by legislation.

clauses.text: Any matter certified by the Court of Appeal under clause (3)(b) may be reviewed by the Supreme Court, and either affirmed, varied or overturned.
The Supreme Court may give an advisory opinion at the request of the national government, any State organ, or any county government with respect to any matter concerning county government.
All courts, other than the Supreme Court, are bound by the decisions of the Supreme Court.
The Supreme Court shall make rules for the exercise of its jurisdiction.
The Supreme Court shall have its own seal.

articles.simple_explanation: The Supreme Court is the highest court in Kenya. It has 7 judges including the Chief Justice (president) and Deputy Chief Justice (deputy president). It has exclusive power to hear presidential election disputes and hears appeals from the Court of Appeal on constitutional matters.

articles.title: Court of Appeal

clauses.text: There is established the Court of Appeal, which—

sub_clauses.text: shall consist of the number of judges, being not fewer than twelve, as may be prescribed by an Act of Parliament; and
shall be organised and administered in the manner prescribed by an Act of Parliament.

clauses.text: There shall be a president of the Court of Appeal who shall be elected by the judges of the Court of Appeal from among themselves.
The Court of Appeal has jurisdiction to hear appeals from—

sub_clauses.text: the High Court; and
any other court or tribunal as prescribed by an Act of Parliament.

articles.simple_explanation: The Court of Appeal hears appeals from the High Court. It has at least 12 judges and its own president elected by the judges.

articles.title: High Court

clauses.text: There is established the High Court, which—

sub_clauses.text: shall consist of the number of judges prescribed by an Act of Parliament; and
shall be organised and administered in the manner prescribed by an Act of Parliament.

clauses.text: There shall be a Principal Judge of the High Court, who shall be elected by the judges of the High Court from among themselves.
Subject to clause (5), the High Court shall have—

sub_clauses.text: unlimited original jurisdiction in criminal and civil matters;
jurisdiction to determine the question whether a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights has been denied, violated, infringed or threatened;
jurisdiction to hear an appeal from a decision of a tribunal appointed under this Constitution to consider the removal of a person from office, other than a tribunal appointed under Article 144;
jurisdiction to hear any question respecting the interpretation of this Constitution including the determination of—

sub_sub_clauses.text: the question whether any law is inconsistent with or in contravention of this Constitution;
the question whether anything said to be done under the authority of this Constitution or of any law is inconsistent with, or in contravention of, this Constitution;
any matter relating to constitutional powers of State organs in respect of county governments and any matter relating to the constitutional relationship between the levels of government; and
a question relating to conflict of laws under Article 191; and

sub_clauses.text: any other jurisdiction, original or appellate, conferred on it by legislation.

clauses.text: Any matter certified by the Court of Appeal under clause (3)(b) may be reviewed by the Supreme Court, and either affirmed, varied or overturned.
The High Court shall not have jurisdiction in respect of matters—

sub_clauses.text: reserved for the exclusive jurisdiction of the Supreme Court under this Constitution; or
falling within the jurisdiction of the courts contemplated in Article 162(2).

clauses.text: The High Court has supervisory jurisdiction over the subordinate courts and over any person, body or authority exercising a judicial or quasi-judicial function, but not over a superior court.

articles.simple_explanation: The High Court has unlimited jurisdiction in criminal and civil matters, and can determine any constitutional question. It supervises all lower courts but cannot hear matters reserved for the Supreme Court.

articles.title: Appointment of Chief Justice, Deputy Chief Justice and other judges

clauses.text: The Chief Justice and the Deputy Chief Justice shall be appointed by the President, subject to the approval of the National Assembly, from among persons recommended by the Judicial Service Commission.
Each judge of a superior court shall be appointed by the President from among persons recommended by the Judicial Service Commission. A person is qualified to be appointed as a judge if the person—

sub_clauses.text: holds a law degree from a recognised university, or is an advocate of the High Court of Kenya, or is qualified to be such an advocate; and

has at least ten years' experience as a superior court judge or professionally qualified magistrate, or as an advocate of the High Court of Kenya, or as a distinguished academic or legal practitioner or such experience in other relevant legal field.

clauses.text: The Chief Justice and the Deputy Chief Justice shall be citizens of Kenya.

The Judicial Service Commission shall not recommend a person to be appointed as a judge unless the Commission has compiled a shortlist of suitable candidates, published their names and invited the public to submit views on them.

articles.simple_explanation: The President appoints the Chief Justice and Deputy Chief Justice with National Assembly approval, from the Judicial Service Commission's recommendations. Judges need at least 10 years' legal experience. The process must be transparent and open to public comment.

articles.title: Tenure of office of the Chief Justice and other judges

clauses.text: A judge shall retire from office on attaining the age of seventy years, but may elect to retire at any time after attaining the age of sixty-five years.
The Chief Justice shall hold office for a term of ten years or until retiring under clause (1), whichever is the earlier, and shall not be eligible for reappointment.
The Deputy Chief Justice, a judge of the Supreme Court or a judge of the Court of Appeal shall hold office until retiring under clause (1), unless the judge—

sub_clauses.text: is removed from office under Article 168; or
otherwise ceases to hold office under this Constitution.

clauses.text: A judge may serve after retirement for such period as may be necessary to enable the judge to deliver judgment or to do any other thing in relation to proceedings that were commenced before the judge before his or her retirement.

articles.simple_explanation: Judges retire at 70 (or can choose to retire at 65). The Chief Justice serves a maximum of one 10-year term. Judges can continue temporarily after retirement to complete cases already before them.

articles.title: Removal from office

clauses.text: A judge of a superior court may be removed from office only on the grounds of—

sub_clauses.text: inability to perform the functions of office arising from mental or physical incapacity;
a breach of a provision of this Constitution or any other law applicable to the conduct of the judge in the performance of the functions of office;
bankruptcy;
incompetence; or
gross misconduct or misbehaviour.

clauses.text: The removal of a judge may be initiated only by the Judicial Service Commission acting on its own motion, or on the petition of any person to the Judicial Service Commission.
A petition by a person to the Judicial Service Commission under clause (2) shall be in writing, setting out the alleged facts constituting the grounds for the judge's removal.
The Judicial Service Commission shall consider the petition and, if it is satisfied that the petition discloses a ground for removal under clause (1), send the petition to the President.
On receiving a petition under clause (4), the President shall—

sub_clauses.text: in the case of a petition against the Chief Justice, appoint a tribunal consisting of—

sub_sub_clauses.text: the Speaker of the National Assembly, as chairperson;
three Supreme Court judges from another Commonwealth jurisdiction; and
one advocate of fifteen years' standing nominated by the Law Society of Kenya; or

sub_clauses.text: in the case of a petition against any other judge, appoint a tribunal consisting of—

sub_sub_clauses.text: a chairperson, who shall be the Chief Justice or a Supreme Court judge designated by the Chief Justice;
three judges from another Commonwealth jurisdiction; and
one advocate of fifteen years' standing nominated by the Law Society of Kenya.

clauses.text: A judge may appeal from the decision of the tribunal to the Supreme Court.

The President shall act on the advice of the Judicial Service Commission in the case of a tribunal for the Chief Justice and the Deputy Chief Justice, and shall suspend the Chief Justice or the Deputy Chief Justice while the matter is being investigated by a tribunal.

articles.simple_explanation: A judge can only be removed for incapacity, breach of the Constitution, bankruptcy, incompetence or gross misconduct. Removal involves a petition to the Judicial Service Commission, a tribunal, and ultimately the President. Judges can appeal their removal to the Supreme Court.

articles.title: Subordinate courts

clauses.text: The subordinate courts are—

sub_clauses.text: the Magistrates courts;
the Kadhis' courts;
the Courts Martial; and
any other court or local tribunal as may be established by an Act of Parliament, other than the courts established under Article 162(2).

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation conferring jurisdiction, functions and powers on the courts established under clause (1).

articles.simple_explanation: Subordinate (lower) courts include Magistrates' Courts, Kadhis' Courts, Courts Martial and any other courts Parliament creates. Parliament sets their jurisdiction and powers.

articles.title: Kadhis' courts

clauses.text: There shall be a Chief Kadhi and such number, being not fewer than three, of other Kadhis as may be prescribed under an Act of Parliament.
A person shall not be appointed to hold or act in the office of Kadhi unless the person—

sub_clauses.text: professes the Muslim religion; and
possesses such knowledge of Muslim law and has such experience in its application as may be prescribed by an Act of Parliament.

clauses.text: The Chief Kadhi and the other Kadhis, or the Chief Kadhi and such of the other Kadhis as may be prescribed by an Act of Parliament, shall each be empowered to hold a Kadhi's court having jurisdiction within Kenya.

The jurisdiction of a Kadhi's court shall be limited to the determination of questions of Muslim law relating to personal status, marriage, divorce or inheritance in proceedings in which all the parties profess the Muslim religion and submit to the jurisdiction of the Kadhi's courts.

articles.simple_explanation: Kadhis' Courts handle personal matters (marriage, divorce, inheritance) for Muslims who voluntarily submit to the court's jurisdiction. All parties must be Muslim. The Chief Kadhi and other Kadhis must be Muslim and knowledgeable in Islamic law.

articles.title: Establishment of the Judicial Service Commission

clauses.text: There is established the Judicial Service Commission.
The Commission shall consist of—

sub_clauses.text: the Chief Justice, who shall be the chairperson of the Commission;
one Supreme Court judge elected by the judges of the Supreme Court;
one Court of Appeal judge elected by the judges of the Court of Appeal;
one High Court judge elected by the judges of the High Court;
the Attorney-General;
two advocates, one of whom shall be of at least fifteen years' standing, elected by the Law Society of Kenya;
one person nominated by the Public Service Commission; and
one woman and one man to represent the public, not being lawyers, appointed by the President with the approval of the National Assembly.

articles.simple_explanation: The Judicial Service Commission oversees the Judiciary. It includes the Chief Justice, one judge from each superior court, the Attorney-General, two advocates, one Public Service Commission nominee, and two public members (one man, one woman) appointed by the President.

articles.title: Functions of the Judicial Service Commission

clauses.text: The Judicial Service Commission shall—

sub_clauses.text: recommend persons for appointment as judges;
review and make recommendations on the conditions of service of—

sub_sub_clauses.text: judges and judicial officers, other than their remuneration; and
the staff of the Judiciary;

sub_clauses.text: appoint, receive complaints against, investigate and remove from office or otherwise discipline registrars, magistrates, other judicial officers and other staff of the Judiciary, in the manner prescribed by an Act of Parliament;
prepare and implement programmes for the continuing education and training of judges and judicial officers; and
advise the national government on improving the efficiency of the administration of justice.

clauses.text: In the performance of its functions, the Commission shall be guided by the following—

sub_clauses.text: competitiveness and transparent processes of appointment of judicial officers and other staff of the Judiciary; and
the promotion of gender equality.

articles.simple_explanation: The JSC recommends judges for appointment, reviews conditions of service, appoints and disciplines magistrates and other judicial officers, and advises the government on improving justice administration.

articles.title: Judiciary Fund

clauses.text: There is established a fund known as the Judiciary Fund, which shall be administered by the Chief Registrar of the Judiciary.
Funds shall be allocated to the Judiciary Fund by an appropriation by the National Assembly.
The Judiciary Fund shall be used to meet the expenses of the Judiciary.

articles.simple_explanation: The Judiciary has its own fund (the Judiciary Fund) managed by the Chief Registrar. Money is allocated by Parliament and used to meet the Judiciary's expenses.

parts.title: Objects and principles of devolved government
Functions and powers of County governments

articles.title: Objects of devolution

clauses.text: The objects of the devolution of government are—

sub_clauses.text: to promote democratic and accountable exercise of power;
to foster national unity by recognising diversity;
to give powers of self-governance to the people and enhance the participation of the people in the exercise of the powers of the State and in making decisions affecting them;
to recognise the right of communities to manage their own affairs and to further their development;
to protect and promote the interests and rights of minorities and marginalised communities;
to promote social and economic development and the provision of proximate, easily accessible services throughout Kenya;
to ensure equitable sharing of national and local resources throughout Kenya;
to facilitate the decentralisation of State organs, their functions and services, from the capital of Kenya; and
to enhance checks and balances and the separation of powers.

articles.simple_explanation: Devolution brings government closer to the people. Its goals include accountability, fostering unity through diversity, empowering communities to manage their own affairs, protecting minorities, equitable resource sharing, and stronger checks and balances.

articles.title: Principles of devolved government

clauses.text: County governments established under this Chapter shall reflect the following principles—

sub_clauses.text: county governments shall be based on democratic principles and the separation of powers;
county governments shall have reliable sources of revenue to enable them to govern and deliver services effectively; and
no more than two-thirds of the members of representative bodies in each county government shall be of the same gender.

articles.simple_explanation: County governments must be democratic, have reliable funding, and ensure no more than two-thirds of their representative bodies are of the same gender.

articles.title: County governments

clauses.text: There shall be a government for each county, consisting of a county assembly and a county executive.
Every county government shall decentralise its functions and the provision of its services to the extent that it is efficient and practicable to do so.

articles.simple_explanation: Each of the 47 counties has its own county assembly (like Parliament) and county executive (like a Cabinet). County governments must take services as close to the people as possible.

articles.title: Membership of county assembly

clauses.text: A county assembly consists of—

sub_clauses.text: members elected by the registered voters of the wards, each ward constituting a single member constituency, on the same day as a general election of Members of Parliament, being the second Tuesday in August, in every fifth year;
the number of special seat members necessary to ensure that no more than two-thirds of the membership of the assembly are of the same gender;
the number of members of marginalised groups, including persons with disabilities and the youth, prescribed by an Act of Parliament; and
the Speaker, who is an ex officio member.

articles.simple_explanation: A county assembly is made up of elected ward members, special seat members to achieve gender balance, members representing marginalised groups, and the Speaker as an ex officio member.

articles.title: Speaker of a county assembly

clauses.text: There shall be a Speaker for each county assembly, who shall be elected by the county assembly from among persons who are not members of the assembly.
A person is not qualified for election as Speaker of a county assembly unless the person—

sub_clauses.text: is a citizen of Kenya;
holds a degree from a university recognised in Kenya; and
satisfies any educational and moral requirements prescribed by this Constitution or by an Act of Parliament.

articles.simple_explanation: Each county assembly elects a Speaker from outside its membership. The Speaker must be a Kenyan citizen with a university degree.

articles.title: County executive committees

clauses.text: The executive authority of the county is vested in the county executive committee.
The county executive committee consists of—

sub_clauses.text: the county governor and the deputy county governor; and
members appointed by the county governor, with the approval of the county assembly, from among persons who are not members of the assembly.

clauses.text: The county governor is the chairperson of the county executive committee.
The members of the county executive committee—

sub_clauses.text: are accountable to the county governor for the exercise of their powers and the performance of their functions; and
act in accordance with the directions of the county governor.

clauses.text: No more than two-thirds of the members of the county executive committee shall be of the same gender.

articles.simple_explanation: The county executive committee runs the county. It includes the governor, deputy governor and other members appointed by the governor with county assembly approval. The governor chairs it. No more than two-thirds can be of the same gender.

articles.title: Election of county governor and deputy county governor

clauses.text: The county governor shall be elected by the registered voters of the county on the same day as a general election of Members of Parliament.
Each candidate for election as county governor shall nominate a person who is qualified for nomination for election as county governor as a candidate for deputy county governor.
A candidate for county governor shall be—

sub_clauses.text: a citizen of Kenya;
a voter in the county;
qualified for election as member of the county assembly; and
nominated by a registered political party, or is an independent candidate who satisfies the requirements of section 85.

clauses.text: A candidate shall be declared elected as county governor if the candidate receives more than half of all the votes cast in the election.
If no candidate is elected, a fresh election shall be held within thirty days after the previous election.

articles.simple_explanation: The county governor is directly elected by registered voters in the county. To win, a candidate needs more than 50% of votes. If no one wins, a run-off is held within 30 days.

articles.title: Removal of a county governor

clauses.text: A county governor may be removed from office on any of the following grounds—

sub_clauses.text: gross violation of this Constitution or any other law;
where there are serious reasons for believing that the county governor has committed a crime under national or international law;
abuse of office or gross misconduct; or
physical or mental incapacity to perform the functions of office.

clauses.text: The removal process is initiated by a petition to the county assembly, setting out the alleged grounds for removal, and signed by at least one-quarter of all the members of the county assembly.

The county assembly shall consider the petition and, if it is supported by a resolution of at least two-thirds of the members of the county assembly, the Speaker of the county assembly shall inform the Speaker of the Senate.
The Senate shall investigate the matter and, if it passes a resolution supported by at least two-thirds of all senators present and voting, the governor shall be removed.

articles.simple_explanation: A county governor can be removed for gross violation of the Constitution, committing a crime, abuse of office or incapacity. The process involves a petition to the county assembly, then the Senate.

articles.title: Vacancy in the office of county governor

clauses.text: The office of county governor shall become vacant if the holder—

sub_clauses.text: dies;
resigns from office;
ceases to be eligible to be elected as county governor; or
is otherwise removed from office under this Constitution.

clauses.text: If a vacancy occurs in the office of county governor, the deputy county governor shall assume the office for the remainder of the term.

If the offices of both the county governor and deputy county governor are vacant, or if the deputy county governor is unable to assume the office, the county executive committee member as designated by the county assembly shall assume the office.

articles.simple_explanation: If the governor's office is vacant (death, resignation or removal), the deputy governor takes over. If both offices are vacant, the county assembly designates an executive committee member to take charge.

articles.title: Functions of county executive committees

clauses.text: A county executive committee shall—

sub_clauses.text: implement county legislation;
implement, within the county, national legislation to the extent that the legislation so requires;
manage and coordinate the functions of the county administration and its departments; and
perform any other functions conferred on it by this Constitution or national legislation.

clauses.text: A county executive committee member shall attend before a committee of the county assembly when required to do so.
Every county executive committee shall make regular reports to the county assembly on the matters under its authority.

articles.simple_explanation: County executive committees implement county laws and national laws at county level, manage county departments, and must report regularly to the county assembly.

articles.title: Urban areas and cities

clauses.text: National legislation shall provide for the governance and management of urban areas and cities and shall, in particular, provide for—

sub_clauses.text: the criteria for establishing urban areas and cities;
the principles of governance and management of urban areas and cities; and
participation by residents in the governance of urban areas and cities.

articles.simple_explanation: National legislation must set out how cities and urban areas within counties are governed and managed, including criteria for establishing them and how residents participate.

articles.title: Legislative authority of county assemblies

clauses.text: The legislative authority of a county is vested in, and exercised by, its county assembly.
A county assembly may make any laws that are necessary for, or incidental to, the effective performance of the functions and exercise of the powers of the county government under the Fourth Schedule.
A county assembly, while respecting the principle of the separation of powers, may exercise oversight over the county executive committee and any other county executive organs.
A county assembly may receive and approve plans and policies for—

sub_clauses.text: the management and exploitation of the county's resources; and
the development and management of its infrastructure and institutions.

articles.simple_explanation: County assemblies make laws for the county, oversee the county executive, and approve plans for managing county resources and infrastructure.

articles.title: Respective functions and powers of national and county governments

clauses.text: Except as otherwise provided by this Constitution, the functions and powers of the national government and the county governments, respectively, are as set out in the Fourth Schedule.
A function or power that is conferred on both levels of government is a function or power within the concurrent jurisdiction of the national and county governments.
A function or power not assigned by this Constitution or national legislation to a county is a function or power of the national government.

articles.simple_explanation: Functions of the national and county governments are set out in the Fourth Schedule. If a function is not assigned to counties, it belongs to the national government.

articles.title: Transfer of functions and powers between levels of government

clauses.text: A function or power of government at one level may be transferred to a government at the other level by agreement between the governments if—

sub_clauses.text: the function or power would be more effectively performed or exercised by the receiving government; and
the transfer of the function or power is not prohibited by the Constitution or national legislation.

clauses.text: If a function or power is transferred from a government at one level to a government at the other level—

sub_clauses.text: arrangements shall be made for the transfer of the staff, assets and liabilities related to the function or power; and
the transfer takes effect when notice of the transfer has been published in the Gazette.

articles.simple_explanation: A function can be transferred between the national and county governments by agreement if the receiving government can handle it better. Staff, assets and liabilities are also transferred.

articles.title: Boundaries of counties

clauses.text: The boundaries of the counties are as provided in the First Schedule.
The boundaries of a county may be altered by an Act of Parliament only on the recommendation of the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission after a public participation process.

articles.simple_explanation: County boundaries are as set in the First Schedule. They can only be changed by Parliament based on a recommendation from the IEBC after public participation.

articles.title: Cooperation between national and county governments

clauses.text: Government at either level shall—

sub_clauses.text: perform its functions, and exercise its powers, in a manner that respects the functional and institutional integrity of government at the other level, and respects the constitutional status and institutions of government at the other level;
assist, support and consult and, as appropriate, implement the legislation of the other level of government; and
liaise with government at the other level for the purpose of exchanging information, coordinating policies and administration and enhancing capacity.

clauses.text: Government at either level shall not exercise its executive authority in a manner that—

sub_clauses.text: impedes or prejudices the exercise of the executive authority of the other level of government; or
is contrary to the national interest.

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation providing for mechanisms and procedures for the national and county governments to resolve inter-governmental disputes.

articles.simple_explanation: National and county governments must work together, respect each other's functions, share information and coordinate policies. Neither can exercise its power in a way that undermines the other.

articles.title: Support for county governments

clauses.text: The national government shall—

sub_clauses.text: assist counties to develop their capacity to govern and provide services; and
support counties in building capacity for self-governance.

clauses.text: Parliament may enact legislation providing for—

sub_clauses.text: a county to receive assistance from the national government or another county; and
conditions on which such assistance may be provided.

clauses.text: If a county government is unable to perform its functions because of lack of capacity, the national government shall intervene to the extent necessary to maintain national standards or economic unity, but the national government shall not exercise the intervention in a manner that unduly undermines the authority of the county government.

articles.simple_explanation: The national government must help counties develop capacity to govern and deliver services. If a county can't perform its functions, the national government can step in — but must not undermine county authority.

articles.title: Conflict of laws

clauses.text: This Article applies if there is an inconsistency between national and county legislation on—

sub_clauses.text: matters within the concurrent jurisdiction of the national and county governments under the Fourth Schedule; or
any other matter.

clauses.text: National legislation prevails over county legislation if the national legislation—

sub_clauses.text: applies uniformly throughout Kenya and any limitation on the jurisdiction of counties that the legislation creates is necessary for any of the purposes mentioned in clause (4);
is aimed at preventing unreasonable action by a county that is prejudicial to the economic, health or security interests of another county or the nation; or
is necessary for the maintenance of national security; the economic unity of Kenya; the protection of the common market; or the protection of a right or fundamental freedom in the Bill of Rights.

clauses.text: County legislation prevails over national legislation if neither clause (2)(a) nor (2)(b) applies to the national legislation.
The following are purposes for which clause (2)(a) applies—

sub_clauses.text: maintaining national security;
maintaining economic unity;
protecting the common market in respect of the movement of goods, services, capital or labour;
promoting equal opportunity or equal access to government services;
protecting the environment;
promoting and protecting intellectual property rights;
promoting and protecting the rights of minorities and marginalised groups; or
promoting the free flow of information and the freedom of communication.

articles.simple_explanation: If national and county laws conflict, national law generally prevails — especially on national security, economic unity, the common market, or fundamental rights. But county law prevails if none of those reasons apply.

articles.title: Suspension of a county government

clauses.text: The President, acting on the recommendation of the Senate, may suspend a county government—

sub_clauses.text: in an emergency arising from internal conflict or war; or
following a declaration of a state of emergency.

clauses.text: The President shall, with the approval of the Senate, appoint an administrator to manage the affairs of the county during the period of suspension.
A suspension under this Article shall be for a period not exceeding ninety days at any one time.
The Senate may, by a resolution, extend a suspension for a further period not exceeding ninety days.

articles.simple_explanation: The President, on Senate recommendation, can suspend a county government during internal conflict or a state of emergency. The suspension can last up to 90 days and can be extended once by the Senate.

articles.title: Qualifications for election as member of county assembly

clauses.text: Unless disqualified under clause (2), a person is eligible for election or nomination as a member of a county assembly if the person—

sub_clauses.text: is a citizen of Kenya;
is a registered voter;
satisfies any educational and moral requirements prescribed by this Constitution or by an Act of Parliament; and
is nominated by a registered political party, or is an independent candidate who satisfies the requirements of section 85.

clauses.text: A person is disqualified from being elected a member of a county assembly if the person—

sub_clauses.text: is a State officer or other public officer, other than a member of a county assembly;
has, at any time within the five years immediately before the date of election, held office as a member of the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission;
is of unsound mind;
is an undischarged bankrupt;
is subject to a sentence of imprisonment of at least six months; or
has been found, in accordance with any law, to have misused or abused a State office or public office.

articles.simple_explanation: To stand for county assembly you must be a citizen, registered voter, meet educational and moral standards, and be nominated by a party or stand as independent. Disqualifications are similar to those for Parliament.

articles.title: Vacation of office of member of county assembly

clauses.text: The office of a member of a county assembly becomes vacant—

sub_clauses.text: if the member dies;
if the member is absent from eight sittings of the assembly without its permission and is unable to offer a satisfactory explanation for the absence;
if the member ceases to be a citizen of Kenya;
if the member is convicted of an offence punishable by imprisonment for at least six months;
on resignation, in writing, addressed to the Speaker of the county assembly; or
if the member is disqualified from election as a member of a county assembly.

articles.simple_explanation: A county assembly member loses their seat for the same reasons as an MP — death, prolonged absence, criminal conviction, resignation, loss of citizenship or disqualification.

articles.title: County assembly power to summon witnesses

clauses.text: A county assembly may summon any person to appear before it or its committees for the purpose of giving evidence or providing information.
For the purposes of this Article, a county assembly has the same powers as the High Court to—

sub_clauses.text: enforce the attendance of witnesses and examine them on oath, affirmation or otherwise;
compel the production of documents; and
issue a commission or request to examine witnesses abroad.

articles.simple_explanation: County assemblies can summon witnesses and compel evidence, similar to the High Court.

articles.title: Public participation and county assembly powers, privileges and immunities

clauses.text: A county assembly shall—

sub_clauses.text: conduct its business in an open manner, and its sittings and those of its committees shall be open to the public; and
facilitate public participation and involvement in the legislative and other business of the assembly and its committees.

clauses.text: A county assembly may not exclude the public from a sitting of a committee unless in exceptional circumstances and the Speaker considers that there are justifiable reasons for the exclusion.
Parliament shall enact legislation providing for the powers, privileges and immunities of—

sub_clauses.text: county assemblies;
committees of county assemblies; and
members of county assemblies.

articles.simple_explanation: County assembly sittings must be open to the public, and public participation must be facilitated. Parliament must set out the privileges and immunities of county assemblies and their members.

articles.title: County assembly gender balance and diversity

clauses.text: Not more than two-thirds of the members of a county assembly elected under Article 177(1)(a) shall be of the same gender.
Parliament shall enact legislation to ensure that—

sub_clauses.text: the membership referred to in Article 177(1)(b) and (c) constitutes not less than one-third of the total membership of the county assembly; and
the membership referred to in Article 177(1)(b) and (c) takes into account the need for gender balance.

articles.simple_explanation: No more than two-thirds of ward members elected to a county assembly can be of the same gender. Parliament ensures special seat members make up at least one-third of total membership.

articles.title: County government during transition

clauses.text: Pending the establishment of a county government in a county, the functions of the county executive committee and the county assembly shall be exercised by national government.
The national government shall take steps to establish the county government.

articles.simple_explanation: Until a county government is established, the national government performs county functions and must take steps to set up county government as soon as possible.

articles.title: Publication of county legislation

clauses.text: A county assembly Bill assented to by the county governor shall be published in the Gazette.
County legislation comes into force on the publication in the Gazette.

articles.simple_explanation: County laws signed by the governor must be published in the Gazette and come into force upon publication.

articles.title: Legislation on Chapter

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation providing for all matters necessary or expedient for giving effect to this Chapter.
Legislation under clause (1) may—

sub_clauses.text: permit a county to perform a function or exercise a power that is—

sub_sub_clauses.text: not assigned to a county by this Constitution; and
not assigned to a State organ or public officer by this Constitution; or

sub_clauses.text: prohibit a county from performing a function or exercising a power that is not assigned to it by this Constitution.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament must pass laws to give full effect to the devolution chapter, including permitting counties to take on additional functions or prohibiting certain activities.

parts.title: Principles and framework of public finance

articles.title: Principles of public finance

clauses.text: The following principles shall guide all aspects of public finance in the Republic—

sub_clauses.text: there shall be openness and accountability, including public participation in financial matters;
the public finance system shall promote an equitable society, and in particular—

sub_sub_clauses.text: the burden of taxation shall be shared fairly;
revenue raised nationally shall be shared equitably among national and county governments; and
expenditure shall promote the equitable development of the country, including by making special provision for marginalised groups and areas;

sub_clauses.text: the burdens and benefits of the use of resources and public borrowing shall be shared equitably between present and future generations;
public money shall be used in a prudent and responsible way; and
financial management shall be responsible, and fiscal reporting shall be clear.

articles.simple_explanation: All public finance must be open, accountable, fair and prudent. Tax burdens must be shared fairly. Revenue must be shared equitably. Public money must be used wisely. Future generations must not be burdened unfairly.

articles.title: Equitable sharing of national revenue

clauses.text: Revenue raised nationally shall be shared equitably among the national and county governments.
County governments may be given additional allocations from the national government's share of the revenue, either conditionally or unconditionally.

articles.simple_explanation: Revenue collected nationally must be shared equitably between the national and county governments. Counties can also receive additional conditional or unconditional allocations from the national share.

articles.title: Equitable share and other financial laws

clauses.text: The following criteria shall be taken into account in determining the equitable shares provided for under Article 202 and in all national legislation concerning county government financing—

sub_clauses.text: the national interest;
any provision that must be made in respect of the public debt and other national obligations;
the needs of the national government, determined by objective criteria;
the need to ensure that county governments are able to perform the functions allocated to them;
the fiscal capacity and efficiency of county governments;
developmental and other needs of counties;
economic disparities within and among counties and the need to remedy them;
the need for affirmative action in respect of disadvantaged areas and groups;
the need for economic optimisation of each county and to provide incentives for each county to optimise its capacity to raise revenue; and
the desirability of stable and predictable allocations of revenue.

clauses.text: For every financial year, the equitable share of the revenue raised nationally that is allocated to county governments shall be not less than fifteen per cent of all revenue collected by the national government.
The amount referred to in clause (2) shall be calculated on the basis of the most recent audited accounts of revenue received, as approved by the National Assembly.

articles.simple_explanation: When deciding how much money goes to counties, the criteria include national needs, county capacity, economic disparities, population, and development needs. Counties must receive at least 15% of nationally collected revenue every year.

articles.title: Equalisation Fund

clauses.text: There is established an Equalisation Fund into which shall be paid one half per cent of all the revenue collected by the national government each year calculated on the basis of the most recent audited accounts of revenue received, as approved by the National Assembly.

The national government shall use the Equalisation Fund only to provide basic services including water, roads, health facilities and electricity to marginalised areas to the extent necessary to bring the quality of those services in those areas to the level generally enjoyed by the rest of the nation, so far as possible.
The national government may use the Equalisation Fund—

sub_clauses.text: to provide the services directly; or
to give funds to county governments on condition that the funds are used to provide the services.

clauses.text: The Equalisation Fund shall not be used for recurrent expenditure.

articles.simple_explanation: Half a percent (0.5%) of national revenue goes into an Equalisation Fund every year. This money is used exclusively to bring basic services (water, roads, health, electricity) in marginalised areas up to the national standard. It cannot be used for salaries or running costs.

articles.title: Consultation on financial legislation affecting counties

clauses.text: When a Bill that includes provisions affecting the finances of county governments is published, the Commission on Revenue Allocation shall consider those provisions and may make recommendations to the National Assembly and the Senate.

If the National Assembly proceeds contrary to the recommendations of the Commission on Revenue Allocation under clause (1), the Speaker of the National Assembly shall notify the Senate, and the Senate shall consider the Bill.

articles.simple_explanation: When Parliament makes financial laws affecting counties, the Commission on Revenue Allocation must be consulted. If the National Assembly ignores the Commission's recommendations, the Senate must consider the matter.

articles.title: Consolidated Fund and other public funds

clauses.text: There is established the Consolidated Fund into which shall be paid all money raised or received by or on behalf of the national government, except money that—

sub_clauses.text: is reasonably excluded from the Consolidated Fund by an Act of Parliament and payable into another public fund established for a specific purpose; or
may, under an Act of Parliament, be retained by the State organ that received it for the purposes of defraying the expenses of the organ.

clauses.text: Money shall not be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund unless—

sub_clauses.text: the withdrawal is authorised by an appropriation by an Act of Parliament; or
it is money charged on the Consolidated Fund by this Constitution or an Act of Parliament.

articles.simple_explanation: All money raised by the national government goes into the Consolidated Fund. Money can only be withdrawn from this fund if Parliament has approved it (appropriated it) or if it is charged to the fund by the Constitution.

articles.title: Revenue Funds for county governments

clauses.text: There shall be established for each county a Revenue Fund into which shall be paid all money raised or received by or on behalf of the county government.
Money may be withdrawn from a Revenue Fund of a county only—

sub_clauses.text: as authorised by an appropriation by a law of the county assembly; or
if the money is charged on that Fund by this Constitution or an Act of Parliament.

clauses.text: Money that is—

sub_clauses.text: held in a Revenue Fund of a county;
allocated to a county government by the national government; or
derived from loans raised by a county government,

clauses.text: ...shall not be regarded as part of the Consolidated Fund.

articles.simple_explanation: Each county has its own Revenue Fund. County money can only be withdrawn through county assembly appropriation. County funds are separate from the national Consolidated Fund.

articles.title: Contingencies Fund

clauses.text: There is established a Contingencies Fund, the operation of which shall be prescribed by an Act of Parliament.
The Cabinet Secretary responsible for finance may authorise a withdrawal from the Contingencies Fund if—

sub_clauses.text: the Cabinet Secretary is satisfied that there is an urgent and unforeseen need for expenditure for which there is no other lawful authority; and
the expenditure cannot be deferred without serious prejudice to the public interest.

clauses.text: As soon as practicable after a withdrawal under clause (2), a supplementary estimate shall be presented to the National Assembly for approval.

articles.simple_explanation: The Contingencies Fund handles urgent, unforeseen expenses when Parliament has not yet approved the spending. The Cabinet Secretary for Finance can authorise withdrawals but Parliament must be informed through a supplementary estimate.

articles.title: Power to impose taxes and charges

clauses.text: Only the national government may impose—

sub_clauses.text: income tax;
value-added tax;
customs duties and other duties on import and export goods; and
excise tax.

clauses.text: An Act of Parliament may authorise the national government to impose any other tax or duty, except a tax specified in clause (3)(a) or (b).
A county may impose—

sub_clauses.text: property rates;
entertainment taxes; and
any other tax that it is authorised to impose by an Act of Parliament.

clauses.text: The national and county governments may impose charges for the services they provide.
The national government may not impose a charge for a service provided by a county government, and a county government may not impose a charge for a service provided by the national government.

articles.simple_explanation: Only the national government can impose income tax, VAT, customs duties and excise tax. Counties can impose property rates, entertainment taxes, and any other taxes Parliament allows. Both governments can charge for their services.

articles.title: Imposition of tax

clauses.text: No tax or licensing fee may be imposed, waived or varied except as provided by legislation.
No law may exclude or authorise the exclusion of a State officer from payment of tax by reason of—

sub_clauses.text: the office held by that State officer; or
the nature of the work of the State officer.

clauses.text: Parliament may, by legislation, exempt any state organ from paying a tax.

articles.simple_explanation: No tax can be imposed, changed or waived without legislation. No State officer is exempt from paying tax because of their position. Parliament can exempt State organs from tax.

articles.title: Borrowing by national government

clauses.text: The national government may borrow money, grant loans and guarantee loans if—

sub_clauses.text: there is a sufficient reason;
the terms are not more than those generally available in the market;
the borrowing or guarantee is not against the national interest; and
in the case of a guarantee, the guarantee is issued and the guarantor is informed that it is issued, under this Constitution.

clauses.text: An Act of Parliament shall provide for the terms on which the national government may borrow.

articles.simple_explanation: The national government can borrow money, give loans and guarantee loans, subject to good reason, market terms, national interest and Parliamentary approval through legislation.

articles.title: Borrowing by counties

clauses.text: A county government may borrow only—

sub_clauses.text: if the national government guarantees the loan; and
with the approval of the county assembly.

articles.simple_explanation: A county government can only borrow money if the national government guarantees the loan AND the county assembly approves it.

articles.title: Loan guarantees by national government

clauses.text: The national government may guarantee loans made to counties, but only with the approval of the National Assembly.
An Act of Parliament shall provide for the terms on which the national government may guarantee loans.

articles.simple_explanation: The national government can only guarantee county loans with National Assembly approval. Parliament must set conditions for such guarantees.

articles.title: Public debt

clauses.text: The public debt of the national government shall be a charge on the Consolidated Fund.

For the purposes of this Article, the public debt includes the interest on that debt, sinking fund payments in respect of that debt and the costs, charges and expenses incidental to the management of that debt.

articles.simple_explanation: The national public debt is charged on the Consolidated Fund. This includes interest, sinking fund payments and all costs of managing the debt.

articles.title: Commission on Revenue Allocation

clauses.text: There is established the Commission on Revenue Allocation.
The Commission shall consist of—

sub_clauses.text: a chairperson;
two persons nominated by the national government;
one person nominated by the county governments; and
one person to represent other interests.

clauses.text: The members of the Commission shall represent the interests of the national government, the county governments and the public.

articles.simple_explanation: The Commission on Revenue Allocation is an independent body responsible for recommending how national revenue is shared between the national government and counties, and among counties.

articles.title: Functions of the Commission on Revenue Allocation

clauses.text: The principal function of the Commission on Revenue Allocation is to make recommendations concerning the basis for the equitable sharing of revenue raised by the national government—

sub_clauses.text: between the national and county governments; and
among the county governments.

clauses.text: The Commission shall also—

sub_clauses.text: make recommendations on matters concerning the financing of, and financial management by, county governments, including the criteria for determining creditworthiness;
investigate and report on the finances of county governments; and
define and enhance the revenue sources of the national and county governments.

clauses.text: In formulating recommendations, the Commission shall—

sub_clauses.text: seek representations from the national government, county governments and the public; and
take into account the criteria set out in Article 203(1).

articles.simple_explanation: The Commission recommends the basis for revenue sharing, advises on county financial management, investigates county finances, and defines revenue sources for both levels of government.

articles.title: Division of revenue

clauses.text: Once every five years, the Senate shall, by resolution, determine the basis for allocating among the counties the share of national revenue that is annually allocated to the county level of government.
In determining the basis of allocation under clause (1), the Senate shall—

sub_clauses.text: take into account the criteria mentioned in Article 203(1); and
request and take into account recommendations made by the Commission on Revenue Allocation.

clauses.text: The Senate may review the basis of allocation before the end of the five year period—

sub_clauses.text: following the publication of a new population census; or
if necessary to comply with the criteria mentioned in Article 203(1).

clauses.text: When the Senate makes a determination under this Article, the determination shall be—

sub_clauses.text: tabled in the National Assembly; and
submitted to the President for assent.

articles.simple_explanation: The Senate decides every 5 years how to share national revenue among the 47 counties. This decision is guided by the Commission on Revenue Allocation and factors like population, poverty levels and infrastructure.

articles.title: Annual Division and Allocation of Revenue Bills

clauses.text: At least two months before the end of each financial year, there shall be introduced in Parliament—

sub_clauses.text: a Division of Revenue Bill, which shall divide revenue raised by the national government among the national and county levels of government in accordance with this Constitution; and

a County Allocation of Revenue Bill, which shall divide among the counties the revenue allocated to the county level of government on the basis determined in accordance with the resolution of the Senate under Article 217.

clauses.text: The Bills described in clause (1) shall be the first Bills introduced in Parliament in each year following the publication of the Division of Revenue and County Allocation of Revenue Bills.

articles.simple_explanation: At least two months before the end of each financial year, two Bills must be introduced: the Division of Revenue Bill (splitting money between national and counties) and the County Allocation of Revenue Bill (splitting county share among all 47 counties).

articles.title: Transfer of equitable share

clauses.text: A county's share of national revenue shall be transferred to the county without undue delay and without deduction, except when the transfer has been stopped under Article 225.

articles.simple_explanation: Each county's share of national revenue must be transferred promptly and in full. The only exception is if it has been stopped due to financial non-compliance (under Article 225).

articles.title: Form, content and timing of budgets

clauses.text: The national government's budget shall be presented to the National Assembly as set out in this Chapter, and shall—

sub_clauses.text: have regard to—

sub_sub_clauses.text: the estimates of revenue and expenditure;
proposals for financing any anticipated deficit for the period to which it relates; and
proposals regarding borrowing and other forms of public liability that will increase public debt during the following year;

sub_clauses.text: be divided into two parts, the first of which shall deal with the expenditure not charged on the Consolidated Fund and the second of which shall deal with other expenditure; and
show the expenditure by programmes, in each case identifying the amounts to be charged on the Consolidated Fund and the amounts to be appropriated by the National Assembly.

clauses.text: Parliament shall, by legislation—

sub_clauses.text: prescribe the form of budgets and financial statements;
prescribe when budgets shall be tabled;
provide for the information that is required to accompany budgets; and
prescribe the financial reporting and accounting standards to apply to national and county governments.

articles.simple_explanation: The national budget must show revenue estimates, financing plans for any deficit, and borrowing proposals. Parliament sets the format, timing and standards for budgets.

articles.title: Budget estimates and annual Appropriation Bill

clauses.text: At least two months before the end of each financial year, the Cabinet Secretary responsible for finance shall submit to the National Assembly estimates of revenue and expenditure for the following year.
The National Assembly shall consider the estimates submitted under clause (1) and may approve or amend the estimates.

The National Assembly shall consider the estimates only after giving the county governments, the Commission on Revenue Allocation and other bodies required by this Constitution and an Act of Parliament to review and comment on the Division of Revenue Bill and the County Allocation of Revenue Bill an opportunity to review and comment on these Bills and a reasonable time for the National Assembly to consider the comments.
After the National Assembly has approved the estimates of expenditure, it shall pass an appropriation Bill, which shall—

sub_clauses.text: appropriate the money approved by the Assembly for expenditure during the following financial year; and
specify the purpose for and manner in which the appropriated money may be used.

clauses.text: Estimates of expenditure appropriated by an appropriation Act for a financial year shall not include expenditure charged on the Consolidated Fund by this Constitution.

articles.simple_explanation: At least two months before the end of each financial year, the Cabinet Secretary for Finance submits estimates to the National Assembly, which can approve or amend them. An Appropriation Bill authorising the spending must then be passed.

articles.title: Expenditure before annual budget is passed

clauses.text: If the appropriation Act for a financial year has not been assented to, or is not likely to be assented to, by the beginning of that financial year, the National Assembly may, by resolution, authorise withdrawals from the Consolidated Fund for the purpose of meeting expenditure necessary to carry on the services of the national government for a period not exceeding four months or until the Act comes into force, whichever is the earlier.
Withdrawals under this Article shall not exceed one-half of the amounts included in the estimates of expenditure for the previous financial year.

articles.simple_explanation: If the budget is not ready by the start of the financial year, the National Assembly can allow the government to spend up to half of the previous year's budget for up to 4 months while it finalises the new budget.

articles.title: Supplementary appropriation

clauses.text: Subject to clause (2), the national government may spend money that has not been appropriated if—

sub_clauses.text: the amount appropriated for any purpose under the appropriation Act is insufficient or a need has arisen for expenditure for a purpose for which no amount has been appropriated by that Act; and
the expenditure has been approved by the Cabinet.

clauses.text: The total of unappropriated expenditure—

sub_clauses.text: under clause (1) shall not exceed two per cent of the sum appropriated by the National Assembly in the most recent appropriation Act; and
shall be included in the next appropriation Bill for ratification by the National Assembly.

articles.simple_explanation: The government can spend up to 2% of the approved budget on urgent unforeseen needs without prior parliamentary approval, but this must be included in the next Appropriation Bill for Parliament to ratify.

articles.title: County appropriation Bills

clauses.text: Each county government shall prepare and adopt its own budget and appropriation legislation in accordance with this Chapter, and shall do so in a manner consistent with the Division of Revenue Bill and the County Allocation of Revenue Bill.

articles.simple_explanation: Each county must also prepare and pass its own annual budget and appropriation law, consistent with the national Division and Allocation of Revenue Bills.

articles.title: Financial control

clauses.text: An Act of Parliament shall provide for the establishment, management and operations of financial control mechanisms for the national and county governments.
Legislation under clause (1) shall provide measures to—

sub_clauses.text: deal with the situation of a county government not complying with the financial controls;
stop the transfer of funds from the national government to a county government that is in serious material breach of the measures prescribed under this Article; and
define material breach for the purposes of paragraph (b).

clauses.text: The Cabinet Secretary responsible for finance shall stop the transfer of funds to a county government only—

sub_clauses.text: with the approval of the Senate, which shall consider the matter within twenty-one days of receiving it; and
if the county government has been given reasonable opportunity to respond.

articles.simple_explanation: National legislation must set up financial controls for both levels of government. The national government can stop transferring money to a county only with Senate approval and after giving the county a chance to respond.

articles.title: Accounts and audit of public entities

clauses.text: Accounts of all national and county government entities shall be audited by the Auditor-General.
Parliament and county assemblies shall enact legislation on the keeping of financial records and audit of accounts of all entities mentioned in this Article.
A person who is responsible for the financial management of a public entity shall ensure that the resources of the entity are used—

sub_clauses.text: in a manner that is lawful and authorised; and
in an effective, efficient, economical and transparent manner.

clauses.text: If the Auditor-General is not satisfied with the financial management of any public entity, the Auditor-General shall report that to the relevant legislature.

articles.simple_explanation: All national and county accounts must be audited by the Auditor-General. Public officers managing money must use it lawfully, effectively and transparently. If the Auditor-General is unsatisfied, they report to the relevant legislature.

articles.title: Procurement of public goods and services

clauses.text: When the national or county government, including any organ of any such government, contracts for goods or services, it shall do so in accordance with a system that is fair, equitable, transparent, competitive and cost-effective.
An Act of Parliament shall prescribe a framework within which policies for procurement and asset disposal shall be implemented and may authorise procedures including—

sub_clauses.text: preference of the goods and services of persons and communities that have been previously disadvantaged by unfair competition or discrimination;
the protection or advancement of persons, categories of persons or groups that are previously disadvantaged;
the implementation of national development goals.

articles.simple_explanation: All government procurement (buying goods and services) must be fair, equitable, transparent, competitive and cost-effective. Parliament sets procurement rules that can include affirmative action for previously disadvantaged groups.

articles.title: Controller of Budget

clauses.text: There shall be a Controller of Budget who shall be nominated by the President and, with the approval of the National Assembly, appointed by the President.
The Controller of Budget shall oversee the implementation of the budgets of the national and county governments by authorising withdrawals from public funds under this Constitution.
The Controller of Budget shall not approve any withdrawal from a public fund unless satisfied that the withdrawal is authorised by law.
Every four months, the Controller of Budget shall submit to each House of Parliament a report on the implementation of the budgets of the national and county governments.
The Controller of Budget shall—

sub_clauses.text: investigate and report on the implementation of the budget of any national or county government entity; and
report to the relevant county assembly on the implementation of the county government's budget.

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation to give effect to this Article.

articles.simple_explanation: The Controller of Budget oversees how national and county budgets are implemented. They must approve all withdrawals from public funds and report to Parliament every four months.

articles.title: Auditor-General

clauses.text: There shall be an Auditor-General who shall be nominated by the President and, with the approval of the National Assembly, appointed by the President.
Within six months after the end of each financial year, the Auditor-General shall audit and report, in respect of that financial year, on—

sub_clauses.text: the accounts of the national and county governments;
the accounts of all funds and authorities of the national and county governments;
the accounts of all courts;
the accounts of every commission and independent office established by this Constitution;
the accounts of the National Assembly, the Senate and the county assemblies;
the accounts of political parties funded from public funds;
the public debt; and
the accounts of any other entity that legislation requires the Auditor-General to audit.

clauses.text: The Auditor-General may audit and report on the accounts of any entity that is funded from public funds.
An audit report shall confirm whether or not public money has been applied lawfully and in an effective way.
Audit reports shall be submitted to the relevant legislature.
Within three months after receiving an audit report, the relevant legislature shall debate and consider the report and take appropriate action.
The Auditor-General shall hold office for a single term of eight years and shall not be eligible for re-appointment.

articles.simple_explanation: The Auditor-General independently audits all government accounts, courts, commissions, political party funds and public debt. Reports must be submitted within 6 months of financial year end. The Auditor-General serves one 8-year term.

articles.title: Salaries and Remuneration Commission

clauses.text: There is established the Salaries and Remuneration Commission.
The Salaries and Remuneration Commission shall consist of—

sub_clauses.text: a chairperson;
one person each nominated by the following bodies from among their members—

sub_sub_clauses.text: the Parliamentary Service Commission;
the Public Service Commission;
the Judicial Service Commission;
the Teachers Service Commission;
the National Police Service Commission;
the Defence Council; and
the Independent Offices and Commissions;

sub_clauses.text: three persons to represent the interests of—

sub_sub_clauses.text: employees;
employers; and
the public.

clauses.text: The functions of the Salaries and Remuneration Commission are to—

sub_clauses.text: set and regularly review the remuneration and benefits of all State officers; and
advise the national and county governments on the remuneration and benefits of all other public officers.

clauses.text: In performing its functions, the Commission shall take into account—

sub_clauses.text: the need to ensure that the total public compensation bill is fiscally sustainable;
the need to ensure that the public service is able to attract and retain the skills it requires to deliver high quality services;
the need to recognise productivity and performance; and
transparency and fairness.

clauses.text: The remuneration and benefits of a State officer shall be as set by the Salaries and Remuneration Commission.
The Commission shall report annually to the National Assembly on the execution of its functions.

articles.simple_explanation: The Salaries and Remuneration Commission sets the pay and benefits of all State officers. It must consider fiscal sustainability, public service needs, performance and fairness. It reports annually to the National Assembly.

articles.title: Central Bank of Kenya

clauses.text: There is established the Central Bank of Kenya.
The Central Bank of Kenya—

sub_clauses.text: is responsible for formulating monetary policy;
is responsible for promoting price stability;
issues currency; and
performs any other functions conferred on it by national legislation.

clauses.text: There shall be a Monetary Policy Committee of the Central Bank of Kenya.
The Central Bank of Kenya shall not be under the direction or control of any person or authority in the exercise of its powers or the performance of its functions.
The Governor of the Central Bank of Kenya shall be appointed by the President with the approval of the National Assembly.
An Act of Parliament shall provide for the composition, powers, functions and operations of the Central Bank of Kenya.

articles.simple_explanation: The Central Bank of Kenya manages monetary policy, promotes price stability and issues currency. It is independent and cannot be directed by any person or authority. Its Governor is appointed by the President with National Assembly approval.

parts.title: Values and principles of public service

articles.title: Values and principles of public service

clauses.text: The values and principles of public service include—

sub_clauses.text: high standards of professional ethics;
efficient, effective and economic use of resources;
responsive, prompt, effective, impartial and equitable provision of services;
involvement of the people in the process of policy making;
accountability for administrative acts;
transparency and provision to the public of timely, accurate information;
subject to paragraphs (h) and (i), fair competition and merit as the basis of appointments and promotions;
representation of Kenya's diverse communities; and
affording adequate and equal opportunities for appointment, training and advancement, at all levels of the public service, of—

sub_sub_clauses.text: men and women;
the members of all ethnic groups; and
persons with disabilities.

clauses.text: These values and principles apply to public service in—

sub_clauses.text: all State organs in both levels of government; and
all State corporations.

clauses.text: Parliament shall enact legislation to give full effect to this Article.

articles.simple_explanation: Public servants must maintain high professional ethics, use resources efficiently, provide services impartially, be accountable and transparent, and be recruited fairly based on merit. The public service must reflect Kenya's diversity and provide equal opportunities for men, women, all ethnic groups and persons with disabilities.

articles.title: The Public Service Commission

clauses.text: There is established the Public Service Commission.
The Commission shall consist of a chairperson, a vice-chairperson and seven other members appointed by the President with the approval of the National Assembly.
A member of the Commission shall be a person of high moral character, proven integrity and with knowledge and experience in public affairs.
The composition of the Commission shall reflect the regional and ethnic diversity of the people of Kenya.

articles.simple_explanation: The Public Service Commission is an independent body with a chairperson, vice-chairperson and 7 members appointed by the President with National Assembly approval. Its composition must reflect Kenya's regional and ethnic diversity.

articles.title: Functions and powers of the Public Service Commission

clauses.text: The Commission shall—

sub_clauses.text: establish and abolish offices in the public service;
appoint persons to hold or act in public offices, and to confirm appointments;
exercise disciplinary control over and remove persons holding or acting in those offices;
promote the values and principles referred to in Articles 10 and 232 throughout the public service;
investigate, monitor and evaluate the organisation, administration and personnel practices of the public service;
ensure that the public service is efficient and effective;
develop human resources in the public service;
review and make recommendations to the national government in respect of conditions of service, code of conduct and qualifications of officers in the national public service;
evaluate and report to the President and Parliament on the extent to which the values and principles referred to in Articles 10 and 232 are complied with in the public service;
hear and determine appeals in respect of county governments' public service; and
perform any other functions and exercise any other powers conferred by national legislation.

clauses.text: The Commission shall not appoint a person under clause (1)(b) to an office on the personal staff of the President, the Deputy President, or a county governor, except with the consent of the President, Deputy President, or governor, as the case may be.

articles.simple_explanation: The PSC hires, promotes, disciplines and fires public servants. It promotes the values of good governance, evaluates public service performance, and hears appeals from county public service decisions.

articles.title: Staffing of county governments

clauses.text: A county government is responsible, within a framework of uniform norms and standards prescribed by an Act of Parliament, for—

sub_clauses.text: establishing and abolishing offices in its public service;
appointing persons to hold or act in those offices; and
exercising disciplinary control over and removing persons holding or acting in those offices.

articles.simple_explanation: Each county government is responsible for creating and managing its own public service — hiring, managing and firing staff — within uniform national standards set by Parliament.

articles.title: Protection of public officers

clauses.text: A public officer shall not be—

sub_clauses.text: victimised or discriminated against for having performed the functions of office in good faith; or
dismissed, removed from office, demoted in rank or otherwise subjected to disciplinary action without due process of law.

articles.simple_explanation: Public servants cannot be victimised or discriminated against for doing their job honestly. They cannot be fired or demoted without proper legal process.

articles.title: Teachers Service Commission

clauses.text: There is established the Teachers Service Commission.
The functions of the Teachers Service Commission are to—

sub_clauses.text: register trained teachers;
recruit and employ registered teachers;
assign teachers employed by it to relevant Public Service Commission positions;
promote and transfer teachers;
exercise disciplinary control over teachers; and
terminate the employment of teachers.

clauses.text: The Commission shall review the standards of education and training of persons entering the teaching service.
The Commission shall advise the national government on matters relating to the teaching profession.

articles.simple_explanation: The Teachers Service Commission registers teachers, employs and deploys them, promotes, transfers and disciplines them. It advises the government on the teaching profession.

articles.title: Principles of national security

clauses.text: National security is the protection against internal and external threats to Kenya's territorial integrity and sovereignty, its people, their rights, freedoms, property, peace, stability and prosperity, and other national interests.
The national security of Kenya shall be promoted and guaranteed in accordance with the following principles—

sub_clauses.text: national security is subject to the authority of this Constitution and Parliament;
national security shall be pursued in compliance with the law and with the utmost respect for the rule of law, democracy, human rights and fundamental freedoms;
in performing their functions and exercising their powers, national security organs shall respect the diverse culture of the communities within Kenya; and
recruitment by the national security organs shall reflect the diversity of the Kenyan people in equitable proportions.

articles.simple_explanation: National security means protecting Kenya's territory, sovereignty, people, rights and interests from internal and external threats. Security must be pursued lawfully, respecting human rights, democracy and Kenya's cultural diversity.

articles.title: National security organs

clauses.text: The national security organs are—

sub_clauses.text: the Kenya Defence Forces;
the National Intelligence Service; and
the National Police Service.

clauses.text: The national security organs and every member of the national security organs shall, in performing their functions and exercising their powers—

sub_clauses.text: act in accordance with this Constitution; and
respect the rule of law and fundamental rights and freedoms of individuals.

clauses.text: A person shall not establish a military, paramilitary, or similar organisation that purports to have a military function, or a political organisation that has a military wing, except as provided for in this Constitution.
The national security organs shall not be used to—

sub_clauses.text: further partisan political interests of any political organisation; or
subvert this Constitution or unlawfully undermine a lawfully established government.

clauses.text: Civil authorities shall have final control over the national security organs.

articles.simple_explanation: Kenya's three national security organs are the Kenya Defence Forces, the National Intelligence Service, and the National Police Service.
