They must act within the law, respect rights and not be used for partisan political purposes. Civilians have final control over security organs.

articles.title: Establishment of the National Security Council

clauses.text: There is established a National Security Council.
The Council consists of—

sub_clauses.text: the President;
the Deputy President;
the Cabinet Secretary responsible for Defence;
the Cabinet Secretary responsible for Foreign Affairs;
the Cabinet Secretary responsible for Internal Security;
the Attorney-General;
the Chief of Kenya Defence Forces;
the Director-General of the National Intelligence Service; and
the Inspector-General of the National Police Service.

clauses.text: The National Security Council shall—

sub_clauses.text: integrate the domestic, foreign and military policies relating to national security in order to enable the national security organs to co-operate and function effectively;
assess and appraise the objectives, commitments and risks to the Republic in respect of actual and potential national security capabilities;
consider policies on matters relating to national security; and
perform any other functions prescribed by national legislation.

clauses.text: The Council shall report annually to Parliament on the state of security of Kenya.

articles.simple_explanation: The National Security Council coordinates national security policy. It includes the President, Deputy President, relevant Cabinet Secretaries, Attorney-General, Defence Forces Chief, Intelligence Director-General and Inspector-General of Police. It reports annually to Parliament.

articles.title: Establishment of Kenya Defence Forces and Defence Council

clauses.text: There are established the Kenya Defence Forces.
The Defence Forces consist of—

sub_clauses.text: the Kenya Army;
the Kenya Air Force; and
the Kenya Navy.

clauses.text: The Defence Forces—

sub_clauses.text: are responsible for the defence and protection of the sovereignty and territorial integrity of the Republic;
shall assist and cooperate with other authorities in situations of emergency or disaster, and report to the National Assembly whenever deployed in such circumstances;
may be deployed to restore peace in any part of Kenya affected by unrest or instability only with the approval of the National Assembly; and
shall not be deployed outside Kenya, except—

sub_sub_clauses.text: on diplomatic mission;
to participate in a regional or international peace keeping mission; or
in pursuit of national security objectives.

clauses.text: The President shall report to Parliament whenever the Defence Forces are deployed in any of the circumstances mentioned in clause (3)(b), (c) and (d).
There is established a Defence Council.
The Defence Council consists of—

sub_clauses.text: the Cabinet Secretary responsible for Defence, who is the chairperson;
the Chief of the Kenya Defence Forces;
the three commanders of the Defence Forces; and
the Principal Secretary in the Ministry responsible for Defence.

clauses.text: The Defence Council—

sub_clauses.text: is responsible for the overall policy, control and supervision of the Kenya Defence Forces; and
performs any other functions prescribed by national legislation.

articles.simple_explanation: The Kenya Defence Forces consist of the Army, Air Force and Navy. They protect Kenya's sovereignty and territory. They can assist in emergencies, restore peace domestically only with National Assembly approval, and be deployed abroad only for diplomatic missions, peacekeeping or national security. The President must report to Parliament on all deployments.

articles.title: Establishment of National Intelligence Service

clauses.text: There is established a National Intelligence Service.
The National Intelligence Service—

sub_clauses.text: is responsible for security intelligence and counterintelligence to enhance national security in accordance with this Constitution; and
performs any other functions prescribed by national legislation.

articles.simple_explanation: The National Intelligence Service gathers security intelligence and conducts counterintelligence to protect national security. It performs any other functions set by legislation.

articles.title: Establishment of the National Police Service

clauses.text: There is established a National Police Service.
The National Police Service consists of—

sub_clauses.text: the Kenya Police Service; and
the Administration Police Service.

articles.simple_explanation: The National Police Service consists of the Kenya Police Service and the Administration Police Service.

articles.title: Objects and functions of the National Police Service

clauses.text: The National Police Service shall—

sub_clauses.text: strive for the highest standards of professionalism and discipline among its members;
prevent corruption and promote and practise transparency and accountability;
comply with constitutional standards of human rights and fundamental freedoms;
train staff to the highest possible standards of competence and integrity and to respect human rights and fundamental freedoms and dignity; and
foster and promote relationships with the broader society.

articles.simple_explanation: The National Police Service must maintain high professionalism, prevent corruption, respect human rights, and build good relations with the public.

articles.title: Command of the National Police Service

clauses.text: There is an Inspector-General of the National Police Service who shall be appointed by the President with the approval of the National Assembly.
The Inspector-General—

sub_clauses.text: is appointed for a single term of four years and is not eligible for re-appointment; and
may be removed from office by the President, subject to clauses (4) and (5).

clauses.text: The Inspector-General shall exercise independent command over the National Police Service, and perform any other functions prescribed by national legislation.
The Inspector-General may be removed from office on the grounds of—

sub_clauses.text: inability to perform the functions of office arising from mental or physical incapacity;
a breach of a provision of this Constitution; or
gross misconduct.

clauses.text: The Cabinet Secretary responsible for police services may give the Inspector-General lawful written directions concerning matters of policy relating to the National Police Service, and the Inspector-General shall comply with those directions.
The Inspector-General shall not comply with a direction given under clause (5) if it would require the Inspector-General to violate this Constitution or any other law.
The Inspector-General shall give the Cabinet Secretary responsible for police services a written explanation for not complying with any direction.

articles.simple_explanation: The Inspector-General commands the National Police Service and is appointed by the President with National Assembly approval. They serve one 4-year term. The Cabinet Secretary for police can give lawful written policy directions, but cannot direct the IG to violate the Constitution.

articles.title: National Police Service Commission

clauses.text: There is established a National Police Service Commission.
The Commission consists of—

sub_clauses.text: the chairperson;
the Inspector-General of the National Police Service;
the Deputy Inspector-General of the Kenya Police Service;
the Deputy Inspector-General of the Administration Police Service; and
three independent members.

clauses.text: The Commission shall—

sub_clauses.text: recruit and appoint persons to hold or act in offices in the service, confirm appointments, and determine promotions and transfers within the National Police Service;
observing due process, exercise disciplinary control over and remove from office persons holding or acting in offices within the Service; and
perform any other functions prescribed by national legislation.

articles.simple_explanation: The National Police Service Commission recruits, promotes, transfers and disciplines police officers. It includes the Inspector-General, both Deputy Inspector-Generals and three independent members.

articles.title: Other police services

clauses.text: Parliament may enact legislation establishing other police services under the supervision of the National Police Service and each county government.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament can create other police services supervised by the National Police Service and county governments.

articles.title: Application of Chapter

clauses.text: This Chapter applies to the commissions and independent offices specified in clause (2), but not to commissions provided for in Chapter Ten or to the National Police Service Commission.
The commissions and independent offices to which this Chapter applies are—

sub_clauses.text: the Kenya National Human Rights and Equality Commission;
the National Land Commission;
the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission;
the Commission on Revenue Allocation;
the Public Service Commission;
the Salaries and Remuneration Commission;
the Teachers Service Commission;
the National Cohesion and Integration Commission;
the Ethics and Anti-Corruption Commission;
the Commission on Administrative Justice;
the Auditor-General;
the Controller of Budget; and
the Director of Public Prosecutions.

articles.simple_explanation: This chapter covers all major independent commissions and offices in Kenya — from the IEBC and Human Rights Commission to the Auditor-General and Director of Public Prosecutions. It does not cover Judiciary commissions or the National Police Service Commission.

articles.title: Objects, authority and funding of commissions and independent offices

clauses.text: The objects of the commissions and the independent offices are to—

sub_clauses.text: protect the sovereignty of the people;
secure the observance by all State organs of democratic values and principles; and
promote constitutionalism.

clauses.text: The commissions and the holders of independent offices—

sub_clauses.text: are subject only to this Constitution and the law; and
are independent and not subject to direction or control by any person or authority.

clauses.text: Parliament shall allocate adequate funds to enable each commission and independent office to perform its functions, and shall ensure that each commission and independent office is accountable to the National Assembly.

articles.simple_explanation: All commissions and independent offices exist to protect the people's sovereignty, ensure government follows democratic values, and promote constitutionalism. They are independent — no one can direct or control them. Parliament must fund them adequately.

articles.title: Composition, appointment and terms of office

clauses.text: Each commission shall consist of at least three, but not more than nine, members.
The chairperson and each member of a commission, and the holder of an independent office, shall be—

sub_clauses.text: identified and recommended through a transparent process;
approved by the National Assembly; and
appointed by the President.

clauses.text: To be appointed, a person shall have the specific qualifications required by this Constitution or national legislation.
Members of a commission shall be appointed for a single term of six years and shall not be eligible for re-appointment.
The holder of an independent office shall be appointed for a single term of six years and shall not be eligible for re-appointment.
The composition of each commission shall have regard for the principle of gender equity and representation of Kenya's communities.
A member of a commission, or the holder of an independent office—

sub_clauses.text: unless ex officio, shall not hold any other State office or public office or, in the case of a commission member, any position in a political party; and
shall not carry out any other paid work.

clauses.text: If a commission has a chairperson and a vice-chairperson, one of them shall be a woman and one of them shall be a man.

articles.simple_explanation: Each commission must have 3 to 9 members. Members are identified through a transparent process, approved by the National Assembly and appointed by the President. They serve one 6-year term and cannot be reappointed. Gender balance must be considered.

articles.title: Removal from office

clauses.text: A member of a commission (other than an ex officio member), or the holder of an independent office, may be removed from office only for—

sub_clauses.text: serious violation of this Constitution or any other law, including a contravention of Chapter Six;
gross misconduct, whether in the performance of the member's or office holder's functions or otherwise;
physical or mental incapacity to perform the functions of office;
incompetence; or
bankruptcy.

clauses.text: A person desiring the removal of a member of a commission or of a holder of an independent office on any ground specified in clause (1) may present a petition to the National Assembly setting out the alleged facts constituting that ground.

The National Assembly shall consider the petition and, if it is satisfied that it discloses a ground under clause (1), shall pass a resolution supported by at least two-thirds of all the members of the National Assembly directing the President to suspend the member or office holder pending investigation of the matter by a tribunal appointed by the President.
The tribunal shall consist of—

sub_clauses.text: a person who holds or has held office as a judge of a superior court, who shall be the chairperson;
at least two persons who are qualified to be appointed as a High Court judge; and
one other person with relevant experience.

clauses.text: The tribunal shall inquire into the matter and report to the President, within thirty days, whether it finds the alleged ground to have been established.
If the tribunal finds that the alleged ground has been established, it shall recommend to the President that the member or office holder be removed from office.
If the tribunal reports that the alleged ground has been established, the President shall remove the member or office holder from office.

articles.simple_explanation: A commissioner or independent office holder can only be removed for serious constitutional violations, gross misconduct, incapacity, incompetence or bankruptcy. Two-thirds of the National Assembly must agree to start the removal process. A tribunal then investigates and recommends to the President.

articles.title: General functions and powers

clauses.text: Each commission, and each holder of an independent office, shall—

sub_clauses.text: act in accordance with this Constitution;
perform the functions, and exercise the powers, of the commission or office as provided for under this Constitution or national legislation;
subject to this Constitution and legislation, develop policies and regulations to guide the operations of the commission or office; and
recruit its own staff.

clauses.text: A commission may investigate, on its own initiative or on a complaint made by a member of the public, any matter falling within its mandate.

articles.simple_explanation: All commissions and independent offices must act within the Constitution, carry out their mandated functions, make their own policies, and hire their own staff. They can also investigate complaints from the public.

articles.title: Incorporation of commissions and independent offices

clauses.text: Each commission and each independent office is a body corporate with perpetual succession and a seal, and has the capacity to sue and be sued.

articles.simple_explanation: Each commission and independent office is a legal body (body corporate) — it can own property, enter contracts and be sued in its own name. It has perpetual succession meaning it continues even when members change.

articles.title: Reporting by commissions and independent offices

clauses.text: As soon as practicable after the end of each financial year, each commission and each holder of an independent office shall submit a report to the President and to Parliament.
At any time, a commission or the holder of an independent office may submit a report to the President or to Parliament.
Any report submitted under this Article shall be published and publicised.

articles.simple_explanation: Every commission and independent office must submit an annual report to both the President and Parliament. Reports must be published and publicised for the public to see.

articles.title: Amendment of this Constitution

clauses.text: A proposed amendment to this Constitution shall be enacted in the form of a Bill, referred to in this Chapter as a 'Constitution Amendment Bill', and may be introduced in the National Assembly or the Senate.
Except as provided in clause (3), a Constitution Amendment Bill shall—

sub_clauses.text: be read in each House of Parliament;
be approved by a vote of at least two-thirds of all the members of each House; and
be submitted to the President for assent.

clauses.text: If a Constitution Amendment Bill proposes to amend any of the following provisions, it shall, after being passed by Parliament, be submitted to the people of Kenya in a national referendum for approval—

sub_clauses.text: the supremacy of the Constitution;
the territory of Kenya;
the Bill of Rights;
the term of office of the President;
the independence of the Judiciary and the commissions and independent offices;
the functions of Parliament;
the objects, principles and structure of devolved government; or
the provisions of this Chapter.

clauses.text: A Bill referred to the people of Kenya in a referendum shall be approved if at least twenty per cent of the registered voters in each of at least half of the counties vote and the majority of the citizens voting in the referendum approve the Bill.

articles.simple_explanation: To amend the Constitution, a Bill must be passed by at least two-thirds of all members of each House of Parliament. However, if the amendment touches fundamental provisions — such as the Bill of Rights, presidential term limits, the Judiciary's independence, devolution, or the amendment process itself — it must also be approved by the people in a national referendum.

articles.title: Amendment by parliamentary initiative

clauses.text: A Constitution Amendment Bill not intended to be submitted for a referendum under Article 255(3) may be introduced in either House of Parliament and shall—

sub_clauses.text: be read in each House of Parliament at intervals of not less than ninety days;
be approved by a vote of at least two-thirds of all the members of each House; and
be submitted to the President for assent.

clauses.text: If a Constitution Amendment Bill intended to be submitted for a referendum under Article 255(3)—

sub_clauses.text: is passed by both Houses of Parliament and such approval is signified by at least two-thirds of all the members in each House, the Bill shall be submitted to the President for assent after approval by referendum as provided in clause (3) of Article 255; or
fails to be approved by both Houses, the Bill may be revived once only during the same parliamentary term, and shall be treated as if it has just been introduced in that House.

clauses.text: When a Bill that intends to amend the provisions listed in Article 255(3) has been passed by each House of Parliament, the Speaker of the National Assembly shall, within three months of the date of such approval, cause a referendum to be held.
The President shall assent to a Constitution Amendment Bill that has been approved in accordance with Article 255(3) within seven days.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament can initiate a constitutional amendment. Bills not requiring a referendum must be read in each House with at least 90 days between readings, and passed by two-thirds majority. Bills requiring a referendum must also go to the people for approval after Parliament passes them.

articles.title: Amendment by popular initiative

clauses.text: An amendment to this Constitution may be proposed by a popular initiative signed by at least one million registered voters.
A popular initiative for an amendment to this Constitution shall be in the form of a general suggestion or a formulated draft Bill.
If a popular initiative is in the form of a general suggestion, the Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission shall assist in formulating a draft Bill embodying the suggestion.
A draft Bill under clause (2) or (3) shall be submitted to the county assemblies for consideration.
If a majority of the county assemblies approve the draft Bill within three months after it was submitted, the draft Bill shall be submitted to the National Assembly.

If the National Assembly approves the draft Bill with at least two-thirds majority of all the members of the National Assembly and of the Senate, the Bill shall be submitted to the President for assent.
If the draft Bill proposes amendments to this Constitution that would alter the provisions listed in Article 255(3), the draft Bill shall also be submitted to a referendum.

articles.simple_explanation: Ordinary Kenyan citizens can also propose a constitutional amendment if they collect signatures from at least one million registered voters. The proposal goes to county assemblies first — if a majority approve it, it goes to Parliament. If Parliament passes it with two-thirds majority, it goes to the President or a referendum if needed.

articles.title: Enforcement of this Constitution

clauses.text: Every person has the right to institute court proceedings, claiming that this Constitution has been contravened, or is threatened with contravention.
In addition to a person acting in their own interest, court proceedings under clause (1) may be instituted by—

sub_clauses.text: a person acting on behalf of another person who cannot act in their own name;
a person acting as a member of, or in the interest of, a group or class of persons;
a person acting in the public interest; or
an association acting in the interest of one or more of its members.

articles.simple_explanation: Every Kenyan has the right to go to court if they believe the Constitution is being violated or threatened — even on behalf of others or in the public interest.

articles.title: Construing this Constitution

clauses.text: This Constitution shall be interpreted in a manner that—

sub_clauses.text: promotes its purposes, values and principles;
advances the rule of law, and the human rights and fundamental freedoms in the Bill of Rights;
permits the development of the law; and
contributes to good governance.

clauses.text: If there is a conflict between different language versions of this Constitution, the English version prevails.
Every provision of this Constitution shall be construed according to the doctrine of interpretation that the law is always speaking and therefore, among other things—

sub_clauses.text: a function or power conferred by this Constitution may be exercised as occasion requires;
the expression 'national legislation' or other words referring to legislation mean legislation in force at the time the function or power is exercised; and
the expression 'the person' in relation to a position, office or body means the person for the time being occupying that position, office or body.

clauses.text: In this Constitution, if a matter that is to be determined by voting in a house of Parliament is required to be supported by a specified fraction of members, the fraction shall be calculated on the basis of the total number of members of the house (excluding the Speaker).

articles.simple_explanation: The Constitution must be interpreted in a way that promotes its purposes, advances human rights, allows the law to develop, and contributes to good governance. If Kiswahili and English versions conflict, the English version prevails.

articles.title: Interpretation

clauses.text: In this Constitution, unless the context otherwise requires—

definitions.definition: means an individual who has attained the age of eighteen years
includes any measure designed to overcome or ameliorate an inequity or the systemic denial or infringement of a right or fundamental freedom
means a State officer who is not an elected State officer
means an individual who has not attained the age of eighteen years
means a law made by a county assembly
includes any publication and any written, printed, photographic, electronic or other record of information
means the date on which this Constitution came into force
means a State officer who is elected under this Constitution
means the period of twelve months ending on the thirtieth June in each year
includes responsibility and duty
means the official Gazette of the Republic of Kenya
means the Registrar of the Supreme Court, a registrar of the Court of Appeal or of the High Court, a magistrate and any other person designated as a judicial officer by national legislation
includes an Act of Parliament, or county legislation, and sub-ordinate legislation
means legislation made by Parliament
includes sunlight, surface water, ground water, forests, biodiversity, and the airspace
includes affirmation
means any State officer or any other person holding a public office

means an office in the national government, a county government or the public service, if the remuneration and benefits of the office are payable directly from the Consolidated Fund or directly out of money provided by Parliament
means a commission, office, agency or other body established under this Constitution
means any instrument of a legislative nature made under a power conferred by this Constitution or any Act of Parliament

articles.simple_explanation: This article defines key terms used throughout the Constitution — like 'adult' (18+), 'child' (under 18), 'financial year' (July to June), 'public office', 'State organ', 'legislation', and others. It is the Constitution's glossary.

articles.title: Consequential legislation

clauses.text: Parliament shall, as soon as practicable after the effective date, enact legislation required by this Constitution to be enacted to govern or establish various institutions, offices, etc.
Parliament shall enact any legislation required by this Constitution within the period specified, and if no period is specified, within a reasonable time.
If Parliament does not enact legislation in accordance with a constitutional provision requiring it to do so, any person may petition the High Court on the matter.

articles.simple_explanation: Parliament must pass all laws required by the Constitution within the time frames set, or within a reasonable time. If Parliament fails to do so, any citizen can petition the High Court to compel Parliament to act.

articles.title: Transitional and consequential provisions

clauses.text: The transitional and consequential provisions set out in the Sixth Schedule shall take effect on the effective date.
To the extent that anything in the Sixth Schedule is inconsistent with any other provision of this Constitution, the Sixth Schedule shall prevail.

articles.simple_explanation: The transitional provisions in the Sixth Schedule took effect from the day the Constitution came into force. If there is any conflict between the Sixth Schedule and other provisions, the Sixth Schedule prevails during the transition period.

articles.title: Effective Date

clauses.text: This Constitution shall come into force on its promulgation by the President or on the expiry of a period of fourteen days from the date of the publication in the Gazette of the final result of the referendum approving this Constitution, whichever is the earlier.

articles.simple_explanation: The Constitution came into force when the President proclaimed it, or 14 days after the referendum results were published in the Gazette — whichever came first.

articles.title: Repeal of previous Constitution

clauses.text: The former Constitution is repealed on the effective date.

articles.simple_explanation: The old (former) Constitution was fully repealed (cancelled) when this Constitution came into force.

schedules.title: COUNTIES
schedules.description: Counties of Kenya as established under Article 6 of the Constitution
schedules.simple_explanation: This is the official list of Kenya's 47 counties — the 47 units of devolved government created by the 2010 Constitution. Each county has its own government with a governor and county assembly.

schedules.title: NATIONAL SYMBOLS
schedules.description: National symbols of the Republic as provided under Article 9

items.description: The national flag of Kenya consists of three equal horizontal stripes of black, red and green, with the red stripe edged in white. Superimposed in the centre is a Maasai shield and two crossed spears in natural colours.
The national coat of arms bears the national shield and crossed spears as the central motif, supported by two lions. The motto 'Harambee' (meaning 'pulling together') appears on a ribbon at the base.
Ee Mungu Nguvu Yetu / O God of all creation / Bless this our land and nation / Justice be our shield and defender / May we dwell in unity / Peace and liberty / Plenty be found within our borders.

schedules.simple_explanation: This describes Kenya's national symbols — the flag (black, red and green stripes with a Maasai shield and spears), the coat of arms (two lions holding a shield and spears with the motto 'Harambee'), and the national anthem.

schedules.title: OATHS AND AFFIRMATIONS
schedules.description: Oaths and affirmations for State offices as provided under Articles 74, 106(4) and 141(2)

oaths.oath_of_allegiance: I, (name), do swear/affirm that I will be faithful and bear true allegiance to the Republic of Kenya; that I will obey, preserve, protect and defend this Constitution of Kenya, as by law established, and all other laws of the Republic and that I will protect and uphold the sovereignty, integrity and dignity of the people of Kenya. (So help me God.)
oaths.oath_of_office: I, (name), do swear/affirm that I will diligently and conscientiously fulfil the duties and functions of the office of President of Kenya; that I will obey, preserve, protect and defend this Constitution of Kenya, as by law established; and that I will promote and safeguard the rule of law and fundamental rights and freedoms. (So help me God.)

oaths.oath_of_allegiance: I, (name), do swear/affirm that I will be faithful and bear true allegiance to the Republic of Kenya; that I will obey, preserve, protect and defend this Constitution of Kenya, as by law established. (So help me God.)
oaths.oath_of_office: I, (name), do swear/affirm that I will diligently and conscientiously fulfil the duties and functions of the office of Deputy President of Kenya and that I will promote and safeguard the rule of law and fundamental rights and freedoms. (So help me God.)

I, (name), do swear/affirm that I will at all times faithfully perform the duties and responsibilities of Cabinet Secretary; that I will obey, preserve, protect and defend this Constitution of Kenya. (So help me God.)

oaths.oath: I, (name), do swear/affirm that I will support, uphold and defend the Constitution of Kenya; and that I will faithfully and conscientiously discharge the duties of a member of Parliament and serve the people of Kenya. (So help me God.)

I, (name), do swear/affirm that I will truly and faithfully perform the duties of the office of Chief Justice/Judge of (court) of Kenya; that I will uphold this Constitution and the laws of Kenya; and that I will be impartial and will not be guided by personal interest, prejudice, or any improper motive. (So help me God.)

oaths.oath_of_allegiance: I, (name), do swear/affirm that I will be faithful and bear true allegiance to the Republic of Kenya; that I will obey, preserve, protect and defend this Constitution of Kenya as by law established. (So help me God.)
oaths.oath_of_office: I, (name), do swear/affirm that I will diligently and conscientiously fulfil the duties and functions of the County Governor of (county); that I will obey, preserve, protect and defend this Constitution of Kenya as by law established. (So help me God.)

schedules.simple_explanation: These are the official words of the oaths that State officers — the President, Deputy President, Cabinet Secretaries, MPs, Judges and Governors — must say before taking up their positions. The oaths commit them to upholding the Constitution and serving the people of Kenya.

schedules.title: DISTRIBUTION OF FUNCTIONS BETWEEN THE NATIONAL GOVERNMENT AND THE COUNTY GOVERNMENTS

parts.title: Functions of the National Government
functions: Foreign affairs, foreign policy and international trade
The use of international waters and water resources
Immigration and citizenship
The relationship between religion and state
Language policy and the promotion of official and local languages
National defence and the use of the national defence services
Police services, including the setting of standards
Courts
National economic policy and planning
Monetary policy, currency, banking (including central banking), the incorporation and regulation of banking, insurance and financial corporations

National statistics and data on population, the economy, land, agriculture, education, manpower, public finance, science and technology, social welfare and any other statistics that may be prescribed by an Act of Parliament
Intellectual property rights
Labour standards
Consumer protection
Education policy, standards, curricula, examinations and the granting of university charters
Universities, tertiary educational institutions and other institutions of research and higher learning and primary schools, special education, secondary schools and special education institutions
Promotion of sports and sports education

Transport and communications, including in particular—(i) road traffic; (ii) the construction and operation of national trunk roads; (iii) standards for the construction of other roads; (iv) railways; (v) pipelines; (vi) marine navigation; (vii) civil aviation; (viii) space institutions; (ix) telecommunications; (x) radio and television broadcasting; (xi) postal services; (xii) the Nairobi metropolitan area
National public works
Housing policy
General principles of land planning and the co-ordination of planning by the counties

Protection of the environment and natural resources with a view to establishing a durable and sustainable system of development, including, in particular—(i) fishing, hunting and gathering; (ii) protection of animals and wildlife; (iii) water protection, securing sufficient residual water, hydraulic engineering and the safety of dams; (iv) energy policy
National referral health facilities
Disaster management
Ancient and historical monuments of national importance
National elections
Betting, casinos and other forms of gambling
Licensing of firearms
Emergency powers
Capacity building and technical assistance to counties
Public investment
National betting, casinos and other forms of gambling
Tourism policy and development

parts.title: Functions of the County Governments
functions: Agriculture, including—(a) crop and animal husbandry; (b) livestock sale yards; (c) county abattoirs; (d) plant and animal disease control; (e) fisheries

County health services, including, in particular—(a) county health facilities and pharmacies; (b) ambulance services; (c) promotion of primary health care; (d) licensing and control of undertakings that sell food to the public; (e) veterinary services (excluding regulation of the profession); (f) cemeteries, funeral parlours and crematoria; (g) refuse removal, refuse dumps and solid waste disposal
Control of air pollution, noise pollution, other public nuisances and outdoor advertising

Cultural activities, public entertainment and public amenities, including—(a) betting, casinos and other forms of gambling; (b) racing; (c) liquor licensing; (d) cinemas; (e) video shows and hiring; (f) libraries; (g) museums; (h) sports and cultural activities and facilities; (i) county parks, beaches and recreation facilities

County transport, including—(a) county roads; (b) street lighting; (c) traffic and parking; (d) public road transport; (e) ferries and harbours, excluding the regulation of international and national shipping and matters related thereto
Animal control and welfare, including—(a) licensing of dogs; (b) facilities for the accommodation, care and burial of animals
Trade development and regulation, including—(a) markets; (b) trade licences (excluding regulation of professions); (c) fair trading practices; (d) local tourism; (e) cooperative societies
County planning and development, including—(a) statistics; (b) land survey and mapping; (c) boundaries and fencing; (d) housing; (e) electricity and gas reticulation and energy regulation
Pre-primary education, village polytechnics, home craft centres and childcare facilities
Implementation of specific national government policies on natural resources and environmental conservation, including—(a) soil and water conservation; (b) forestry
County public works and services, including—(a) storm water management systems in built-up areas; (b) water and sanitation services
Fire fighting services and disaster management
Control of drugs and pornography

Ensuring and coordinating the participation of communities and locations in governance at the local level and assisting communities and locations to develop the administrative capacity for the effective exercise of the functions and powers and participation in governance at the local level

schedules.simple_explanation: This schedule divides responsibilities between the national government and county governments. Part 1 lists what the national government handles (like defence, immigration, railways, universities, monetary policy). Part 2 lists what counties handle (like local roads, markets, health centres, pre-primary schools, agriculture, refuse collection).

schedules.title: LIMITATIONS ON FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS AND FREEDOMS RELATING TO THE KENYA DEFENCE FORCES AND THE NATIONAL POLICE SERVICE
schedules.content: The following rights and fundamental freedoms may be limited by legislation to the extent strictly necessary for the proper functioning of the Kenya Defence Forces and the National Police Service, and consistent with other provisions of this Constitution: Article 31 (Privacy), Article 36 (Freedom of association), Article 37 (Assembly, demonstration, picketing and petition), Article 41 (Labour relations), Article 43 (Economic and social rights), Article 49 (Rights of arrested persons).
provisions: Article 37 — Assembly, demonstration, picketing and petition
schedules.simple_explanation: This schedule lists the rights (privacy, freedom of association, assembly, labour rights, economic and social rights, and rights of arrested persons) that can be limited for members of the Kenya Defence Forces and the National Police Service — but only to the extent strictly necessary for those institutions to function properly.

schedules.title: TRANSITIONAL AND CONSEQUENTIAL PROVISIONS

key_provisions.heading: Continuation of existing law
key_provisions.text: All law in force immediately before the effective date continues in force and shall be construed with alterations, adaptations, qualifications and exceptions necessary to bring it into conformity with this Constitution.

key_provisions.heading: Existing institutions
key_provisions.text: Any institution established and operating under the former Constitution immediately before the effective date shall continue to operate as if established under this Constitution.

key_provisions.heading: Existing courts
key_provisions.text: Every court established under the former Constitution shall continue to operate and its decisions shall be binding on all courts of equal or lower status, and appeals from decisions of those courts shall lie as they would have lain under the former Constitution.

key_provisions.heading: Pending court proceedings
key_provisions.text: All court proceedings pending immediately before the effective date shall continue as if commenced under this Constitution.

key_provisions.heading: First elections
key_provisions.text: The first elections of members of county assemblies and governors shall be held at the same time as the first general elections held under this Constitution.

key_provisions.heading: Constitutional offices
key_provisions.text: A person who immediately before the effective date held or was acting in an office established by the former Constitution shall continue to hold or act in that office under this Constitution.

key_provisions.heading: Legislation to be enacted
key_provisions.text: Within five years after the effective date, Parliament shall enact legislation providing for all matters necessary or required by this Constitution, including matters set out in the Fifth Schedule.

key_provisions.heading: Vesting of property
key_provisions.text: All property that immediately before the effective date was held by or on behalf of the government of Kenya shall vest in the national government.

key_provisions.heading: County boundaries
key_provisions.text: Until a county is established, the functions of the county government in that county shall be performed by the national government.

schedules.simple_explanation: This schedule managed the changeover from the old constitution to the new 2010 Constitution. It ensured existing laws stayed in force until updated, existing government institutions continued operating, court cases continued, property transferred to the new government structure, and county governments were established over time.
